Hachimangū |例祭 = {{jalink|October 14|Q2922}}-{{jalink|October 15|Q2919}} |神事 = |地図 = Nagoya }} [[File:Takamu shrine, Plaque of shrine name (Chikusa ward, Nagoya city. 2016.03.17).jpg|thumb|220px|right|Shrine-name plaque on the worship hall. "Written by Nobusuke Kishi" appears at lower left.]] [[File:Chikusa Takamu Shrine 2009-08B.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Torii of subsidiary Tokugi Inari Shrine]] [[File:Takamu Shrine, Chōzuya (Chikusa ward, Nagoya city. 2016.03.02).JPG|thumb|220px|right|Temizu basin at Takamu Shrine. Water is drawn from the well in back and used for purification.]] '''Takamu Shrine''' (たかむじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Imaike 1-chōme, {{jalink|Chikusa-ku|Q1207951}}, {{jalink|Nagoya|Q11751}}, {{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}. It is identified with “Takamu Shrine” of Aichi District, {{jalink|Owari Province|Q514506}}, in the '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}'. === History === It is an old shrine traditionally said to have been founded in the era of {{jalink|Emperor Seimu|Q316672}}, with {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}} enshrined as an accompanying deity in the era of {{jalink|Emperor Seiwa|Q349243}}According to the precinct information board.. Until the {{jalink|early modern period|Q5308718}} it was called '''Hachimangū'''. Because it has a {{jalink|sacred statue|Q11588751}} riding a {{jalink|boat|Q1229765}}, it was also called “Funade Hachiman.” Since {{jalink|1441|Q6402}} ({{jalink|Kakitsu|Q1193238}} 1), records of multiple building constructions/repairs are transmitted{{Sfn|名古屋市史|1915年|p=320}}{{Efn|In 'Nagoya City History: Shrines and Temples,' munafuda from Kanshō 2 (1461), Tenbun 1 (1532), Eiroku 8 (1565), and Keichō 8 (1603) existing at compilation time are listed (pp.322-325).}}, but all those {{jalink|munafuda|Q8008834}} call it “Koimura Hachimangū” or “Hachimangū.” There is a tradition that before the {{jalink|Keichō|Q1328185}} era it stood at Hakusan in Genkai Village west of its current site (present Hakusan-sha in {{jalink|Shinsakae|Q11502585}}, {{jalink|Naka-ku, Nagoya|Q1207921|Naka Ward}}), and was moved to the current site during the Kiyosu relocation accompanying the construction of {{jalink|Nagoya Castle|Q648629}}. In the early-Edo '{{jalink|Kanbun Muramura Oboegaki|Q137766629}},' there is an entry “Furui village... Hachiman, formerly tax-exempt, held by Furui village negi Mokusaemon,” showing that in the {{jalink|early modern period|Q5308718}} the Kojima family, negi of Furui Village, served as {{jalink|kannushi|Q15888735}}. A record exists that Owari lord {{jalink|Tokugawa Mitsutomo|Q843245}} repaired the shrine buildings in {{jalink|Tenna|Q1145289|Tenna}} 3 ({{jalink|1683|Q7651}}){{Sfn|千種区の歴史|1981年|p=34}}{{Sfn|千種区史|1987年|p=429}}. Until {{jalink|shinbutsu bunri|Q1970535}}, Gyokuryūzan Kōshō-in (still east of this shrine) was its {{jalink|jingū-ji|Q1074971}}. In {{jalink|1873|Q7757}} (Meiji 5), it was ranked {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} in the {{jalink|modern shrine rank system|Q712534}}. Shrine buildings were burned in air raids during the {{jalink|Pacific War|Q184425}}, and rebuilt after the war. By {{jalink|Amano Nobukage|Q6535030}}, it was identified with “Takamu Shrine” of Aichi District in the 'Engishiki Jinmyōchō,' and this became standard from the mid-Edo period onward. In {{jalink|1868|Q7717}} (Meiji 1) it was officially renamed “Takamu Shrine.” However, the basis for that identification is not clear, and late-Edo local historian {{jalink|Tsuda Masao|Q22123474}} questioned it. Other claim shrines include Takamine Daimyōjin (now merged into {{jalink|Yagoto Shrine}}) and Takamu Shrine in {{jalink|Takahari|Q50324950}}, but both are in the area of {{jalink|Yamada District (Owari)|Q11470699|Yamada District}} before the {{jalink|medieval period|Q12554}}, so they are not viewed as strong candidates for the Aichi District Takamu Shrine. Tsuda Masao proposed {{jalink|Gokiso Hachimangū|Q3109957}} as another candidate. From the {{jalink|Edo period}} onward, a theory spread that “Takamu” is an honorific for {{jalink|hoko spear|Q728}} and that the {{jalink|Mononobe clan|Q1755836}} had an armory hereFrom the historic-site board in the precincts (installed by Nagoya City Board of Education).{{Sfn|千種区の歴史|1981年|p=33}}{{Sfn|千種区史|1987年|p=428}}. But since the late-Heian '{{jalink|Owari no Kuni Naijinmyōchō|Q11041830}}' writes it as “Takamuku Tenjin,” the original reading may have been “Takamuku,” potentially related to the {{jalink|Takamuku clan|Q109357793}} and {{jalink|Takamuku Shrine|Q11668968}}. === Enshrined deities === * {{jalink|Takamimusubi-no-Kami}} (たかみむすびのかみ) * {{jalink|Kamimusubi-no-Kami}} (かみむすびのかみ) * {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}} (Hondawake-no-Mikoto, ほんだわけのみこと) === Subsidiary shrines === The following subsidiary shrines are in the precincts. {{Div col}} * Kitano Tenjin Shrine * Takamu Ryūjin Shrine * Taga Shrine * Kotohira Shrine * Itsukushima Shrine * Shinmei Shrine * Hakusan Shrine * Tsushima Shrine * Tokugi Inari Shrine * Sorei Shrine * Akiba Shrine {{Div col end}} === Other === As part of the '''"Three Shrines for Love Pilgrimage"''', together with {{jalink|Yamada Tenmangū|Q3571407}} (Yamada-chō, {{jalink|Kita-ku, Nagoya|Q1207964|Kita Ward}}) and {{jalink|Shiroyama Hachimangū|Q3482375}} (Shiroyama-chō, Chikusa Ward), it is known as a {{jalink|pilgrimage|Q1644573}} spot for love-fulfillment{{Cite web|ja|date= |url=http://www.nagoya-info.jp/shiru/koinosanshameguri.html |title=Love Three-Shrine Pilgrimage (Nagoya Concierge) |publisher=Nagoya Convention & Visitors Bureau |accessdate=2016-03-18}}{{Cite web|ja|date= |url=http://www.shiroyama.or.jp/sansyameguri.htm |title=Love Three-Shrine Pilgrimage |publisher=Shiroyama Hachimangū |accessdate=2016-03-18}}. It was originally planned in {{jalink|2002|Q1987}} (Heisei 14) as a club activity at {{jalink|Ōka Gakuen High School|Q11538202}}, and became widely known after {{jalink|JR Central|Q513679}} featured it as a walking event in {{jalink|2008|Q2004}} (Heisei 20). Originally the three were {{jalink|Nagoya Seimei Shrine|Q17229502}} (Seimeiyama, Chikusa Ward), Takamu Shrine, and Shiroyama HachimangūTetsuya Hiooka, "Love Three-Shrine Pilgrimage: students of Ōka Gakuen plan event on the 26th," {{jalink|Mainichi Shimbun|Q1136866}}, morning ed., local/Aichi, May 1, 2007, p.20Natsumi Ikai, "Aichi archaeology fun: Takamu Shrine, Chikusa Ward, springwater healing legend," {{jalink|Chunichi Shimbun|Q904678}}, morning ed., Dec. 13, 2008, p.19Ken Shiobara, "Find love by visiting three shrines? Quiet boom from high-school plan, Nagoya Chikusa," {{jalink|Asahi Shimbun|Q720503}}, evening ed. (Nagoya HQ), Feb. 3, 2012, p.7{{Cite web|ja|url=http://opi-rina.chunichi.co.jp/tankentai/20100128_jinja.html |title=Prayers for romance at three love-related shrines (Opi Expedition) |publisher=Chunichi Web |date=2010-01-28 |accessdate=2016-03-18}}. There is an old well in the precincts still used for {{jalink|temizu|Q1069667}}. It is also the origin of the alias “Furui Hachiman,” the former town name “Motofurui-chō,” and nearby place name “Koinosaka.” The shrine-name plaque on the worship hall is calligraphy by {{jalink|Nobusuke Kishi|Q315572}}. === Location === * {{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}{{jalink|Nagoya|Q11751}}{{jalink|Chikusa-ku|Q1207951}}{{jalink|Imaike (Nagoya)|Q11377165|Imaike}} 1-4-18 === Access === * 150 m southeast from {{jalink|Chikusa Station|Q863068}} on the {{jalink|JR Chūō Main Line (Nagoya area)|Q11363767|Chūō Main Line}} / {{jalink|Nagoya Municipal Subway Higashiyama Line|Q1132799|Higashiyama Line}}. * 200 m west from {{jalink|Imaike Station (Aichi)|Q852173|Imaike Station}} on the Higashiyama Line / {{jalink|Nagoya Municipal Subway Sakuradōri Line|Q1132780|Sakuradōri Line}}. === Notes === === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Bibliography === * Precinct information board * Historic-site board (installed by Nagoya City Board of Education) * {{Cite book|ja|editor=Compiled by Nagoya City|author=|year=1915|chapter=|title=Nagoya City History: Shrines and Temples|publisher=Nagoya City Office|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|名古屋市史|1915年}}}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=Chikusa Ward Women's Local History Society|year=1981|chapter=|title=History of Chikusa Ward|publisher=Aichi Prefecture Local Materials Publishing Society|series=Nagoya Ward History Series|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|千種区の歴史|1981年}}}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor=50th Anniversary Committee of Chikusa Ward Administration|author=|year=1987|chapter=|title=Chikusa Ward History: 50th Anniversary Commemorative Volume|publisher=50th Anniversary Committee|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|千種区史|1987年}}}} * {{jalink|Owari Meisho Zue|Q11465290|'Owari Meisho Zue' Vol.5 Takamu Shrine}}, 1844 === External links === * [https://jinja-net.jp/takamujinja/ Takamu Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:たかむしんしや}} [[Category:Weird possibly ai botched translations]] == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata (名古屋市)]] [[Category:Need translation]] [[Category:Qsort]]{{DEFAULTSORT:QTakamu Shrine (Q18233534)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QTakamu Shrine (Q18233534)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Takamu Shrine (Q18233534)}}

But since the late-Heian '{{jalink|Owari no Kuni Naijinmyōchō|Q11041830}}' writes it as “Takamuku Tenjin,” the original reading may have been “Takamuku,” potentially related to the {{jalink|Takamuku clan|Q109357793}} and {{jalink|Takamuku Shrine|Q11668968}}. === Enshrined deities === * {{jalink|Takamimusubi-no-Kami}} (たかみむすびのかみ) * {{jalink|Kamimusubi-no-Kami}} (かみむすびのかみ) * {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}} (Hondawake-no-Mikoto, ほんだわけのみこと) === Subsidiary shrines === The following subsidiary shrines are in the precincts. {{Div col}} * Kitano Tenjin Shrine * Takamu Ryūjin Shrine * Taga Shrine * Kotohira Shrine * Itsukushima Shrine * Shinmei Shrine * Hakusan Shrine * Tsushima Shrine * Tokugi Inari Shrine * Sorei Shrine * Akiba Shrine {{Div col end}} === Other === As part of the '''"Three Shrines for Love Pilgrimage"''', together with {{jalink|Yamada Tenmangū|Q3571407}} (Yamada-chō, {{jalink|Kita-ku, Nagoya|Q1207964|Kita Ward}}) and {{jalink|Shiroyama Hachimangū|Q3482375}} (Shiroyama-chō, Chikusa Ward), it is known as a {{jalink|pilgrimage|Q1644573}} spot for love-fulfillment{{Cite web|ja|date= |url=http://www.nagoya-info.jp/shiru/koinosanshameguri.html |title=Love Three-Shrine Pilgrimage (Nagoya Concierge) |publisher=Nagoya Convention & Visitors Bureau |accessdate=2016-03-18}}{{Cite web|ja|date= |url=http://www.shiroyama.or.jp/sansyameguri.htm |title=Love Three-Shrine Pilgrimage |publisher=Shiroyama Hachimangū |accessdate=2016-03-18}}. It was originally planned in {{jalink|2002|Q1987}} (Heisei 14) as a club activity at {{jalink|Ōka Gakuen High School|Q11538202}}, and became widely known after {{jalink|JR Central|Q513679}} featured it as a walking event in {{jalink|2008|Q2004}} (Heisei 20). Originally the three were {{jalink|Nagoya Seimei Shrine|Q17229502}} (Seimeiyama, Chikusa Ward), Takamu Shrine, and Shiroyama HachimangūTetsuya Hiooka, "Love Three-Shrine Pilgrimage: students of Ōka Gakuen plan event on the 26th," {{jalink|Mainichi Shimbun|Q1136866}}, morning ed., local/Aichi, May 1, 2007, p.20Natsumi Ikai, "Aichi archaeology fun: Takamu Shrine, Chikusa Ward, springwater healing legend," {{jalink|Chunichi Shimbun|Q904678}}, morning ed., Dec. 13, 2008, p.19Ken Shiobara, "Find love by visiting three shrines? Quiet boom from high-school plan, Nagoya Chikusa," {{jalink|Asahi Shimbun|Q720503}}, evening ed. (Nagoya HQ), Feb. 3, 2012, p.7{{Cite web|ja|url=http://opi-rina.chunichi.co.jp/tankentai/20100128_jinja.html |title=Prayers for romance at three love-related shrines (Opi Expedition) |publisher=Chunichi Web |date=2010-01-28 |accessdate=2016-03-18}}. There is an old well in the precincts still used for {{jalink|temizu|Q1069667}}. It is also the origin of the alias “Furui Hachiman,” the former town name “Motofurui-chō,” and nearby place name “Koinosaka.” The shrine-name plaque on the worship hall is calligraphy by {{jalink|Nobusuke Kishi|Q315572}}. === Location === * {{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}{{jalink|Nagoya|Q11751}}{{jalink|Chikusa-ku|Q1207951}}{{jalink|Imaike (Nagoya)|Q11377165|Imaike}} 1-4-18 === Hachimangū |例祭 = {{jalink|October 14|Q2922}}-{{jalink|October 15|Q2919}} |神事 = |地図 = Nagoya }} [[File:Takamu shrine, Plaque of shrine name (Chikusa ward, Nagoya city. 2016.03.17).jpg|thumb|220px|right|Shrine-name plaque on the worship hall. "Written by Nobusuke Kishi" appears at lower left.]] [[File:Chikusa Takamu Shrine 2009-08B.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Torii of subsidiary Tokugi Inari Shrine]] [[File:Takamu Shrine, Chōzuya (Chikusa ward, Nagoya city. 2016.03.02).JPG|thumb|220px|right|Temizu basin at Takamu Shrine. Water is drawn from the well in back and used for purification.]] '''Takamu Shrine''' (たかむじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Imaike 1-chōme, {{jalink|Chikusa-ku|Q1207951}}, {{jalink|Nagoya|Q11751}}, {{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}. It is identified with “Takamu Shrine” of Aichi District, {{jalink|Owari Province|Q514506}}, in the '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}'. === History === It is an old shrine traditionally said to have been founded in the era of {{jalink|Emperor Seimu|Q316672}}, with {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}} enshrined as an accompanying deity in the era of {{jalink|Emperor Seiwa|Q349243}}According to the precinct information board.. Until the {{jalink|early modern period|Q5308718}} it was called '''Hachimangū'''. Because it has a {{jalink|sacred statue|Q11588751}} riding a {{jalink|boat|Q1229765}}, it was also called “Funade Hachiman.” Since {{jalink|1441|Q6402}} ({{jalink|Kakitsu|Q1193238}} 1), records of multiple building constructions/repairs are transmitted{{Sfn|名古屋市史|1915年|p=320}}{{Efn|In 'Nagoya City History: Shrines and Temples,' munafuda from Kanshō 2 (1461), Tenbun 1 (1532), Eiroku 8 (1565), and Keichō 8 (1603) existing at compilation time are listed (pp.322-325).}}, but all those {{jalink|munafuda|Q8008834}} call it “Koimura Hachimangū” or “Hachimangū.” There is a tradition that before the {{jalink|Keichō|Q1328185}} era it stood at Hakusan in Genkai Village west of its current site (present Hakusan-sha in {{jalink|Shinsakae|Q11502585}}, {{jalink|Naka-ku, Nagoya|Q1207921|Naka Ward}}), and was moved to the current site during the Kiyosu relocation accompanying the construction of {{jalink|Nagoya Castle|Q648629}}. In the early-Edo '{{jalink|Kanbun Muramura Oboegaki|Q137766629}},' there is an entry “Furui village... Hachiman, formerly tax-exempt, held by Furui village negi Mokusaemon,” showing that in the {{jalink|early modern period|Q5308718}} the Kojima family, negi of Furui Village, served as {{jalink|kannushi|Q15888735}}. A record exists that Owari lord {{jalink|Tokugawa Mitsutomo|Q843245}} repaired the shrine buildings in {{jalink|Tenna|Q1145289|Tenna}} 3 ({{jalink|1683|Q7651}}){{Sfn|千種区の歴史|1981年|p=34}}{{Sfn|千種区史|1987年|p=429}}. Until {{jalink|shinbutsu bunri|Q1970535}}, Gyokuryūzan Kōshō-in (still east of this shrine) was its {{jalink|jingū-ji|Q1074971}}. In {{jalink|1873|Q7757}} (Meiji 5), it was ranked {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} in the {{jalink|modern shrine rank system|Q712534}}. Shrine buildings were burned in air raids during the {{jalink|Pacific War|Q184425}}, and rebuilt after the war. By {{jalink|Amano Nobukage|Q6535030}}, it was identified with “Takamu Shrine” of Aichi District in the 'Engishiki Jinmyōchō,' and this became standard from the mid-Edo period onward. In {{jalink|1868|Q7717}} (Meiji 1) it was officially renamed “Takamu Shrine.” However, the basis for that identification is not clear, and late-Edo local historian {{jalink|Tsuda Masao|Q22123474}} questioned it. Other claim shrines include Takamine Daimyōjin (now merged into {{jalink|Yagoto Shrine}}) and Takamu Shrine in {{jalink|Takahari|Q50324950}}, but both are in the area of {{jalink|Yamada District (Owari)|Q11470699|Yamada District}} before the {{jalink|medieval period|Q12554}}, so they are not viewed as strong candidates for the Aichi District Takamu Shrine. Tsuda Masao proposed {{jalink|Gokiso Hachimangū|Q3109957}} as another candidate. From the {{jalink|Edo period}} onward, a theory spread that “Takamu” is an honorific for {{jalink|hoko spear|Q728}} and that the {{jalink|Mononobe clan|Q1755836}} had an armory hereFrom the historic-site board in the precincts (installed by Nagoya City Board of Education).{{Sfn|千種区の歴史|1981年|p=33}}{{Sfn|千種区史|1987年|p=428}}. But since the late-Heian '{{jalink|Owari no Kuni Naijinmyōchō|Q11041830}}' writes it as “Takamuku Tenjin,” the original reading may have been “Takamuku,” potentially related to the {{jalink|Takamuku clan|Q109357793}} and {{jalink|Takamuku Shrine|Q11668968}}. === Enshrined deities === * {{jalink|Takamimusubi-no-Kami}} (たかみむすびのかみ) * {{jalink|Kamimusubi-no-Kami}} (かみむすびのかみ) * {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}} (Hondawake-no-Mikoto, ほんだわけのみこと) === Subsidiary shrines === The following subsidiary shrines are in the precincts. {{Div col}} * Kitano Tenjin Shrine * Takamu Ryūjin Shrine * Taga Shrine * Kotohira Shrine * Itsukushima Shrine * Shinmei Shrine * Hakusan Shrine * Tsushima Shrine * Tokugi Inari Shrine * Sorei Shrine * Akiba Shrine {{Div col end}} === Other === As part of the '''"Three Shrines for Love Pilgrimage"''', together with {{jalink|Yamada Tenmangū|Q3571407}} (Yamada-chō, {{jalink|Kita-ku, Nagoya|Q1207964|Kita Ward}}) and {{jalink|Shiroyama Hachimangū|Q3482375}} (Shiroyama-chō, Chikusa Ward), it is known as a {{jalink|pilgrimage|Q1644573}} spot for love-fulfillment{{Cite web|ja|date= |url=http://www.nagoya-info.jp/shiru/koinosanshameguri.html |title=Love Three-Shrine Pilgrimage (Nagoya Concierge) |publisher=Nagoya Convention & Visitors Bureau |accessdate=2016-03-18}}{{Cite web|ja|date= |url=http://www.shiroyama.or.jp/sansyameguri.htm |title=Love Three-Shrine Pilgrimage |publisher=Shiroyama Hachimangū |accessdate=2016-03-18}}. It was originally planned in {{jalink|2002|Q1987}} (Heisei 14) as a club activity at {{jalink|Ōka Gakuen High School|Q11538202}}, and became widely known after {{jalink|JR Central|Q513679}} featured it as a walking event in {{jalink|2008|Q2004}} (Heisei 20). Originally the three were {{jalink|Nagoya Seimei Shrine|Q17229502}} (Seimeiyama, Chikusa Ward), Takamu Shrine, and Shiroyama HachimangūTetsuya Hiooka, "Love Three-Shrine Pilgrimage: students of Ōka Gakuen plan event on the 26th," {{jalink|Mainichi Shimbun|Q1136866}}, morning ed., local/Aichi, May 1, 2007, p.20Natsumi Ikai, "Aichi archaeology fun: Takamu Shrine, Chikusa Ward, springwater healing legend," {{jalink|Chunichi Shimbun|Q904678}}, morning ed., Dec. 13, 2008, p.19Ken Shiobara, "Find love by visiting three shrines? Hachimangū |例祭 = {{jalink|October 14|Q2922}}-{{jalink|October 15|Q2919}} |神事 = |地図 = Nagoya }} [[File:Takamu shrine, Plaque of shrine name (Chikusa ward, Nagoya city. 2016.03.17).jpg|thumb|220px|right|Shrine-name plaque on the worship hall. "Written by Nobusuke Kishi" appears at lower left.]] [[File:Chikusa Takamu Shrine 2009-08B.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Torii of subsidiary Tokugi Inari Shrine]] [[File:Takamu Shrine, Chōzuya (Chikusa ward, Nagoya city. 2016.03.02).JPG|thumb|220px|right|Temizu basin at Takamu Shrine. Water is drawn from the well in back and used for purification.]] '''Takamu Shrine''' (たかむじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Imaike 1-chōme, {{jalink|Chikusa-ku|Q1207951}}, {{jalink|Nagoya|Q11751}}, {{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}. It is identified with “Takamu Shrine” of Aichi District, {{jalink|Owari Province|Q514506}}, in the '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}'. === History === It is an old shrine traditionally said to have been founded in the era of {{jalink|Emperor Seimu|Q316672}}, with {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}} enshrined as an accompanying deity in the era of {{jalink|Emperor Seiwa|Q349243}}According to the precinct information board.. Until the {{jalink|early modern period|Q5308718}} it was called '''Hachimangū'''. Because it has a {{jalink|sacred statue|Q11588751}} riding a {{jalink|boat|Q1229765}}, it was also called “Funade Hachiman.” Since {{jalink|1441|Q6402}} ({{jalink|Kakitsu|Q1193238}} 1), records of multiple building constructions/repairs are transmitted{{Sfn|名古屋市史|1915年|p=320}}{{Efn|In 'Nagoya City History: Shrines and Temples,' munafuda from Kanshō 2 (1461), Tenbun 1 (1532), Eiroku 8 (1565), and Keichō 8 (1603) existing at compilation time are listed (pp.322-325).}}, but all those {{jalink|munafuda|Q8008834}} call it “Koimura Hachimangū” or “Hachimangū.” There is a tradition that before the {{jalink|Keichō|Q1328185}} era it stood at Hakusan in Genkai Village west of its current site (present Hakusan-sha in {{jalink|Shinsakae|Q11502585}}, {{jalink|Naka-ku, Nagoya|Q1207921|Naka Ward}}), and was moved to the current site during the Kiyosu relocation accompanying the construction of {{jalink|Nagoya Castle|Q648629}}. In the early-Edo '{{jalink|Kanbun Muramura Oboegaki|Q137766629}},' there is an entry “Furui village... Hachiman, formerly tax-exempt, held by Furui village negi Mokusaemon,” showing that in the {{jalink|early modern period|Q5308718}} the Kojima family, negi of Furui Village, served as {{jalink|kannushi|Q15888735}}. A record exists that Owari lord {{jalink|Tokugawa Mitsutomo|Q843245}} repaired the shrine buildings in {{jalink|Tenna|Q1145289|Tenna}} 3 ({{jalink|1683|Q7651}}){{Sfn|千種区の歴史|1981年|p=34}}{{Sfn|千種区史|1987年|p=429}}. Until {{jalink|shinbutsu bunri|Q1970535}}, Gyokuryūzan Kōshō-in (still east of this shrine) was its {{jalink|jingū-ji|Q1074971}}. In {{jalink|1873|Q7757}} (Meiji 5), it was ranked {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} in the {{jalink|modern shrine rank system|Q712534}}. Shrine buildings were burned in air raids during the {{jalink|Pacific War|Q184425}}, and rebuilt after the war. By {{jalink|Amano Nobukage|Q6535030}}, it was identified with “Takamu Shrine” of Aichi District in the 'Engishiki Jinmyōchō,' and this became standard from the mid-Edo period onward. In {{jalink|1868|Q7717}} (Meiji 1) it was officially renamed “Takamu Shrine.” However, the basis for that identification is not clear, and late-Edo local historian {{jalink|Tsuda Masao|Q22123474}} questioned it. Other claim shrines include Takamine Daimyōjin (now merged into {{jalink|Yagoto Shrine}}) and Takamu Shrine in {{jalink|Takahari|Q50324950}}, but both are in the area of {{jalink|Yamada District (Owari)|Q11470699|Yamada District}} before the {{jalink|medieval period|Q12554}}, so they are not viewed as strong candidates for the Aichi District Takamu Shrine. Tsuda Masao proposed {{jalink|Gokiso Hachimangū|Q3109957}} as another candidate. From the {{jalink|Edo period}} onward, a theory spread that “Takamu” is an honorific for {{jalink|hoko spear|Q728}} and that the {{jalink|Mononobe clan|Q1755836}} had an armory hereFrom the historic-site board in the precincts (installed by Nagoya City Board of Education).{{Sfn|千種区の歴史|1981年|p=33}}{{Sfn|千種区史|1987年|p=428}}. But since the late-Heian '{{jalink|Owari no Kuni Naijinmyōchō|Q11041830}}' writes it as “Takamuku Tenjin,” the original reading may have been “Takamuku,” potentially related to the {{jalink|Takamuku clan|Q109357793}} and {{jalink|Takamuku Shrine|Q11668968}}. === Enshrined deities === * {{jalink|Takamimusubi-no-Kami}} (たかみむすびのかみ) * {{jalink|Kamimusubi-no-Kami}} (かみむすびのかみ) * {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}} (Hondawake-no-Mikoto, ほんだわけのみこと) === Subsidiary shrines === The following subsidiary shrines are in the precincts. {{Div col}} * Kitano Tenjin Shrine * Takamu Ryūjin Shrine * Taga Shrine * Kotohira Shrine * Itsukushima Shrine * Shinmei Shrine * Hakusan Shrine * Tsushima Shrine * Tokugi Inari Shrine * Sorei Shrine * Akiba Shrine {{Div col end}} === Other === As part of the '''"Three Shrines for Love Pilgrimage"''', together with {{jalink|Yamada Tenmangū|Q3571407}} (Yamada-chō, {{jalink|Kita-ku, Nagoya|Q1207964|Kita Ward}}) and {{jalink|Shiroyama Hachimangū|Q3482375}} (Shiroyama-chō, Chikusa Ward), it is known as a {{jalink|pilgrimage|Q1644573}} spot for love-fulfillment{{Cite web|ja|date= |url=http://www.nagoya-info.jp/shiru/koinosanshameguri.html |title=Love Three-Shrine Pilgrimage (Nagoya Concierge) |publisher=Nagoya Convention & Visitors Bureau |accessdate=2016-03-18}}{{Cite web|ja|date= |url=http://www.shiroyama.or.jp/sansyameguri.htm |title=Love Three-Shrine Pilgrimage |publisher=Shiroyama Hachimangū |accessdate=2016-03-18}}. It was originally planned in {{jalink|2002|Q1987}} (Heisei 14) as a club activity at {{jalink|Ōka Gakuen High School|Q11538202}}, and became widely known after {{jalink|JR Central|Q513679}} featured it as a walking event in {{jalink|2008|Q2004}} (Heisei 20). Originally the three were {{jalink|Nagoya Seimei Shrine|Q17229502}} (Seimeiyama, Chikusa Ward), Takamu Shrine, and Shiroyama HachimangūTetsuya Hiooka, "Love Three-Shrine Pilgrimage: students of Ōka Gakuen plan event on the 26th," {{jalink|Mainichi Shimbun|Q1136866}}, morning ed., local/Aichi, May 1, 2007, p.20Natsumi Ikai, "Aichi archaeology fun: Takamu Shrine, Chikusa Ward, springwater healing legend," {{jalink|Chunichi Shimbun|Q904678}}, morning ed., Dec. 13, 2008, p.19Ken Shiobara, "Find love by visiting three shrines? Quiet boom from high-school plan, Nagoya Chikusa," {{jalink|Asahi Shimbun|Q720503}}, evening ed. (Nagoya HQ), Feb. 3, 2012, p.7{{Cite web|ja|url=http://opi-rina.chunichi.co.jp/tankentai/20100128_jinja.html |title=Prayers for romance at three love-related shrines (Opi Expedition) |publisher=Chunichi Web |date=2010-01-28 |accessdate=2016-03-18}}. There is an old well in the precincts still used for {{jalink|temizu|Q1069667}}. It is also the origin of the alias “Furui Hachiman,” the former town name “Motofurui-chō,” and nearby place name “Koinosaka.” The shrine-name plaque on the worship hall is calligraphy by {{jalink|Nobusuke Kishi|Q315572}}. === Location === * {{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}{{jalink|Nagoya|Q11751}}{{jalink|Chikusa-ku|Q1207951}}{{jalink|Imaike (Nagoya)|Q11377165|Imaike}} 1-4-18 === Access === * 150 m southeast from {{jalink|Chikusa Station|Q863068}} on the {{jalink|JR Chūō Main Line (Nagoya area)|Q11363767|Chūō Main Line}} / {{jalink|Nagoya Municipal Subway Higashiyama Line|Q1132799|Higashiyama Line}}. * 200 m west from {{jalink|Imaike Station (Aichi)|Q852173|Imaike Station}} on the Higashiyama Line / {{jalink|Nagoya Municipal Subway Sakuradōri Line|Q1132780|Sakuradōri Line}}. === Notes === === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Bibliography === * Precinct information board * Historic-site board (installed by Nagoya City Board of Education) * {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |所在地 = {{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}{{jalink|Nagoya|Q11751}}{{jalink|Chikusa-ku|Q1207951}}{{jalink|Imaike (Nagoya)|Q11377165|Imaike}} 1-4-18 | 緯度度 = 35 | 緯度分 = 10 | 緯度秒 = 9.2 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 136 |経度分 = 55 | 経度秒 = 57.1 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | 地図国コード = JP-23 |祭神 = {{jalink|Takamimusubi-no-Kami}}

{{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}} |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor)
{{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} |創建 = (trad.) era of {{jalink|Emperor Seimu|Q316672}} |本殿 = {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |別名 = Furui Hachiman
Funade Hachiman
Hachiman
Hachimangū |例祭 = {{jalink|October 14|Q2922}}-{{jalink|October 15|Q2919}} |神事 = |地図 = Nagoya }} [[File:Takamu shrine, Plaque of shrine name (Chikusa ward, Nagoya city. 2016.03.17).jpg|thumb|220px|right|Shrine-name plaque on the worship hall. "Written by Nobusuke Kishi" appears at lower left.]] [[File:Chikusa Takamu Shrine 2009-08B.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Torii of subsidiary Tokugi Inari Shrine]] [[File:Takamu Shrine, Chōzuya (Chikusa ward, Nagoya city. 2016.03.02).JPG|thumb|220px|right|Temizu basin at Takamu Shrine. Water is drawn from the well in back and used for purification.]] '''Takamu Shrine''' (たかむじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Imaike 1-chōme, {{jalink|Chikusa-ku|Q1207951}}, {{jalink|Nagoya|Q11751}}, {{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}. It is identified with “Takamu Shrine” of Aichi District, {{jalink|Owari Province|Q514506}}, in the '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}'. === History === It is an old shrine traditionally said to have been founded in the era of {{jalink|Emperor Seimu|Q316672}}, with {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}} enshrined as an accompanying deity in the era of {{jalink|Emperor Seiwa|Q349243}}According to the precinct information board.. Until the {{jalink|early modern period|Q5308718}} it was called '''Hachimangū'''. Because it has a {{jalink|sacred statue|Q11588751}} riding a {{jalink|boat|Q1229765}}, it was also called “Funade Hachiman.” Since {{jalink|1441|Q6402}} ({{jalink|Kakitsu|Q1193238}} 1), records of multiple building constructions/repairs are transmitted{{Sfn|名古屋市史|1915年|p=320}}{{Efn|In 'Nagoya City History: Shrines and Temples,' munafuda from Kanshō 2 (1461), Tenbun 1 (1532), Eiroku 8 (1565), and Keichō 8 (1603) existing at compilation time are listed (pp.322-325).}}, but all those {{jalink|munafuda|Q8008834}} call it “Koimura Hachimangū” or “Hachimangū.” There is a tradition that before the {{jalink|Keichō|Q1328185}} era it stood at Hakusan in Genkai Village west of its current site (present Hakusan-sha in {{jalink|Shinsakae|Q11502585}}, {{jalink|Naka-ku, Nagoya|Q1207921|Naka Ward}}), and was moved to the current site during the Kiyosu relocation accompanying the construction of {{jalink|Nagoya Castle|Q648629}}. In the early-Edo '{{jalink|Kanbun Muramura Oboegaki|Q137766629}},' there is an entry “Furui village... Hachiman, formerly tax-exempt, held by Furui village negi Mokusaemon,” showing that in the {{jalink|early modern period|Q5308718}} the Kojima family, negi of Furui Village, served as {{jalink|kannushi|Q15888735}}. A record exists that Owari lord {{jalink|Tokugawa Mitsutomo|Q843245}} repaired the shrine buildings in {{jalink|Tenna|Q1145289|Tenna}} 3 ({{jalink|1683|Q7651}}){{Sfn|千種区の歴史|1981年|p=34}}{{Sfn|千種区史|1987年|p=429}}. Until {{jalink|shinbutsu bunri|Q1970535}}, Gyokuryūzan Kōshō-in (still east of this shrine) was its {{jalink|jingū-ji|Q1074971}}. In {{jalink|1873|Q7757}} (Meiji 5), it was ranked {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} in the {{jalink|modern shrine rank system|Q712534}}. Shrine buildings were burned in air raids during the {{jalink|Pacific War|Q184425}}, and rebuilt after the war. By {{jalink|Amano Nobukage|Q6535030}}, it was identified with “Takamu Shrine” of Aichi District in the 'Engishiki Jinmyōchō,' and this became standard from the mid-Edo period onward. In {{jalink|1868|Q7717}} (Meiji 1) it was officially renamed “Takamu Shrine.” However, the basis for that identification is not clear, and late-Edo local historian {{jalink|Tsuda Masao|Q22123474}} questioned it. Other claim shrines include Takamine Daimyōjin (now merged into {{jalink|Yagoto Shrine}}) and Takamu Shrine in {{jalink|Takahari|Q50324950}}, but both are in the area of {{jalink|Yamada District (Owari)|Q11470699|Yamada District}} before the {{jalink|medieval period|Q12554}}, so they are not viewed as strong candidates for the Aichi District Takamu Shrine. Tsuda Masao proposed {{jalink|Gokiso Hachimangū|Q3109957}} as another candidate. From the {{jalink|Edo period}} onward, a theory spread that “Takamu” is an honorific for {{jalink|hoko spear|Q728}} and that the {{jalink|Mononobe clan|Q1755836}} had an armory hereFrom the historic-site board in the precincts (installed by Nagoya City Board of Education).{{Sfn|千種区の歴史|1981年|p=33}}{{Sfn|千種区史|1987年|p=428}}. But since the late-Heian '{{jalink|Owari no Kuni Naijinmyōchō|Q11041830}}' writes it as “Takamuku Tenjin,” the original reading may have been “Takamuku,” potentially related to the {{jalink|Takamuku clan|Q109357793}} and {{jalink|Takamuku Shrine|Q11668968}}. === Enshrined deities === * {{jalink|Takamimusubi-no-Kami}} (たかみむすびのかみ) * {{jalink|Kamimusubi-no-Kami}} (かみむすびのかみ) * {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}} (Hondawake-no-Mikoto, ほんだわけのみこと) === Subsidiary shrines === The following subsidiary shrines are in the precincts. {{Div col}} * Kitano Tenjin Shrine * Takamu Ryūjin Shrine * Taga Shrine * Kotohira Shrine * Itsukushima Shrine * Shinmei Shrine * Hakusan Shrine * Tsushima Shrine * Tokugi Inari Shrine * Sorei Shrine * Akiba Shrine {{Div col end}} === Other === As part of the '''"Three Shrines for Love Pilgrimage"''', together with {{jalink|Yamada Tenmangū|Q3571407}} (Yamada-chō, {{jalink|Kita-ku, Nagoya|Q1207964|Kita Ward}}) and {{jalink|Shiroyama Hachimangū|Q3482375}} (Shiroyama-chō, Chikusa Ward), it is known as a {{jalink|pilgrimage|Q1644573}} spot for love-fulfillment{{Cite web|ja|date= |url=http://www.nagoya-info.jp/shiru/koinosanshameguri.html |title=Love Three-Shrine Pilgrimage (Nagoya Concierge) |publisher=Nagoya Convention & Visitors Bureau |accessdate=2016-03-18}}{{Cite web|ja|date= |url=http://www.shiroyama.or.jp/sansyameguri.htm |title=Love Three-Shrine Pilgrimage |publisher=Shiroyama Hachimangū |accessdate=2016-03-18}}. It was originally planned in {{jalink|2002|Q1987}} (Heisei 14) as a club activity at {{jalink|Ōka Gakuen High School|Q11538202}}, and became widely known after {{jalink|JR Central|Q513679}} featured it as a walking event in {{jalink|2008|Q2004}} (Heisei 20). Originally the three were {{jalink|Nagoya Seimei Shrine|Q17229502}} (Seimeiyama, Chikusa Ward), Takamu Shrine, and Shiroyama HachimangūTetsuya Hiooka, "Love Three-Shrine Pilgrimage: students of Ōka Gakuen plan event on the 26th," {{jalink|Mainichi Shimbun|Q1136866}}, morning ed., local/Aichi, May 1, 2007, p.20Natsumi Ikai, "Aichi archaeology fun: Takamu Shrine, Chikusa Ward, springwater healing legend," {{jalink|Chunichi Shimbun|Q904678}}, morning ed., Dec. 13, 2008, p.19Ken Shiobara, "Find love by visiting three shrines? Quiet boom from high-school plan, Nagoya Chikusa," {{jalink|Asahi Shimbun|Q720503}}, evening ed. (Nagoya HQ), Feb. 3, 2012, p.7{{Cite web|ja|url=http://opi-rina.chunichi.co.jp/tankentai/20100128_jinja.html |title=Prayers for romance at three love-related shrines (Opi Expedition) |publisher=Chunichi Web |date=2010-01-28 |accessdate=2016-03-18}}. There is an old well in the precincts still used for {{jalink|temizu|Q1069667}}. It is also the origin of the alias “Furui Hachiman,” the former town name “Motofurui-chō,” and nearby place name “Koinosaka.” The shrine-name plaque on the worship hall is calligraphy by {{jalink|Nobusuke Kishi|Q315572}}. === Location === * {{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}{{jalink|Nagoya|Q11751}}{{jalink|Chikusa-ku|Q1207951}}{{jalink|Imaike (Nagoya)|Q11377165|Imaike}} 1-4-18 === Access === * 150 m southeast from {{jalink|Chikusa Station|Q863068}} on the {{jalink|JR Chūō Main Line (Nagoya area)|Q11363767|Chūō Main Line}} / {{jalink|Nagoya Municipal Subway Higashiyama Line|Q1132799|Higashiyama Line}}. * 200 m west from {{jalink|Imaike Station (Aichi)|Q852173|Imaike Station}} on the Higashiyama Line / {{jalink|Nagoya Municipal Subway Sakuradōri Line|Q1132780|Sakuradōri Line}}. === Notes === === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Bibliography === * Precinct information board * Historic-site board (installed by Nagoya City Board of Education) * {{Cite book|ja|editor=Compiled by Nagoya City|author=|year=1915|chapter=|title=Nagoya City History: Shrines and Temples|publisher=Nagoya City Office|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|名古屋市史|1915年}}}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=Chikusa Ward Women's Local History Society|year=1981|chapter=|title=History of Chikusa Ward|publisher=Aichi Prefecture Local Materials Publishing Society|series=Nagoya Ward History Series|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|千種区の歴史|1981年}}}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor=50th Anniversary Committee of Chikusa Ward Administration|author=|year=1987|chapter=|title=Chikusa Ward History: 50th Anniversary Commemorative Volume|publisher=50th Anniversary Committee|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|千種区史|1987年}}}} * {{jalink|Owari Meisho Zue|Q11465290|'Owari Meisho Zue' Vol.5 Takamu Shrine}}, 1844 === 拝殿
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |所在地 = {{jalink|愛知県|Q80434}}{{jalink|名古屋市|Q11751}}{{jalink|千種区|Q1207951}}{{jalink|今池 (名古屋市)|Q11377165|今池}}一丁目4-18 | 緯度度 = 35 | 緯度分 = 10 | 緯度秒 = 9.2 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 136 |経度分 = 55 | 経度秒 = 57.1 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | 地図国コード = JP-23 |祭神 = {{jalink|高皇産霊神}}
{{jalink|神皇産霊神}}
{{jalink|応神天皇|Q317997}} |社格 = {{jalink|式内社}}（小）
{{jalink|郷社|Q134917316}} |創建 = （伝）{{jalink|成務天皇|Q316672}}の時代 |本殿 = {{jalink|流造|Q210684}} |別名 = 古井八幡
船出八幡
八幡
八幡宮 |例祭 = {{jalink|10月14日|Q2922}}-{{jalink|10月15日|Q2919}} |神事 = |地図 = Nagoya }} [[File:Takamu shrine, Plaque of shrine name (Chikusa ward, Nagoya city. 2016.03.17).jpg|thumb|220px|right|拝殿の社号額。左下に「岸信介書」とある。]] [[File:Chikusa Takamu Shrine 2009-08B.JPG|thumb|220px|right|末社・徳義稲荷社の鳥居]] [[File:Takamu Shrine, Chōzuya (Chikusa ward, Nagoya city. 2016.03.02).JPG|thumb|220px|right|高牟神社の手水鉢。奥にある井戸から水を汲み上げ、手水に用いている。]] '''高牟神社'''（たかむじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|愛知県|Q80434}}{{jalink|名古屋市|Q11751}}{{jalink|千種区|Q1207951}}{{jalink|今池 (名古屋市)|Q11377165|今池}}一丁目にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。『{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932}}』の{{jalink|尾張国|Q514506}}{{jalink|愛知郡 (愛知県)|Q552687|愛智郡}}「高牟神社」に比定されている。 === 歴史 === {{jalink|成務天皇|Q316672}}の時代に創建され、{{jalink|清和天皇|Q349243}}の時代に{{jalink|応神天皇|Q317997}}が配祀されたと伝えられる古社である境内説明板による。。{{jalink|近世|Q5308718}}まで'''八幡宮'''と称していた。{{jalink|船|Q1229765}}に乗る{{jalink|神像|Q11588751}}があることから「船出八幡」とも呼ばれた。 {{jalink|1441年|Q6402}}（{{jalink|嘉吉|Q1193238}}元年）以降、複数の社殿造営・修復の記録が伝わるが{{Sfn|名古屋市史|1915年|p=320}}{{Efn|『名古屋市史 社寺編』には、編纂時点で伝来していた{{jalink|寛正|Q1143928}}2年（{{jalink|1461年|Q6532}}）・{{jalink|天文 (元号)|Q1192853|天文}}元年（{{jalink|1532年|Q6436}}）・{{jalink|永禄|Q1143341}}8年（{{jalink|1565年|Q6634}}）・{{jalink|慶長|Q1328185}}8年（{{jalink|1603年|Q6735}}）の棟札が所載されている（p.322-325）。}}、いずれの{{jalink|棟札|Q8008834}}でも「小井村八幡宮」「八幡宮」としている。{{jalink|慶長|Q1328185}}年間以前は現在地より西の玄海村白山（現在の{{jalink|中区 (名古屋市)|Q1207921|中区}}{{jalink|新栄|Q11502585}}の白山社）に所在していたが、{{jalink|名古屋城|Q648629}}築城の{{jalink|清洲越し|Q11561274}}に際して現在地へ移転したという伝がある。江戸前期成立の『{{jalink|寛文村々覚書|Q137766629}}』には「古井村...八幡 前々除 古井村祢宜 杢左衛門持分」とあり、{{jalink|近世|Q5308718}}には古井村の{{jalink|禰宜|Q11594490}}であった小島家が{{jalink|神主|Q15888735}}を務めていた。{{jalink|尾張藩|Q1062072}}主の{{jalink|徳川光友|Q843245}}が{{jalink|天和 (日本)|Q1145289|天和}}3年（{{jalink|1683年|Q7651}}）に社殿を修造した記録がある{{Sfn|千種区の歴史|1981年|p=34}}{{Sfn|千種区史|1987年|p=429}}。{{jalink|神仏分離|Q1970535}}となるまで玉龍山光正院（現在も当社の東に所在）が{{jalink|神宮寺|Q1074971}}であった。{{jalink|1873年|Q7757}}（明治5年）、{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534}}において{{jalink|郷社|Q134917316}}に列した。{{jalink|太平洋戦争|Q184425}}の{{jalink|空襲|Q2380335}}で社殿等は焼失し、戦後再建された。 {{jalink|天野信景|Q6535030}}により『延喜式神名帳』にみえる尾張国愛智郡「高牟神社」に比定され、江戸中期以降は定説となった。{{jalink|1868年|Q7717}}（{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}元年）には正式に「高牟神社」と改称している。しかし当社が高牟神社に比定された根拠は明確ではなく、江戸後期の{{jalink|郷土史家}}である{{jalink|津田正生|Q22123474}}は当社を「高牟神社」に比定することに疑問を呈している。当社以外の高牟神社の論社として高峯大明神（現在は{{jalink|八事神社}}に合祀）や{{jalink|高針|Q50324950}}の高牟神社があるが、これらはいずれも{{jalink|中世|Q12554}}以前の{{jalink|山田郡 (尾張国)|Q11470699|山田郡}}域内であるため愛知郡の高牟神社の候補として有力視されていない。津田正生は高牟神社の候補として他に{{jalink|御器所八幡宮|Q3109957}}を挙げている。 {{jalink|江戸期}}以降、「高牟」は{{jalink|武器|Q728}}の象徴である{{jalink|鉾}}の美称で{{jalink|物部氏|Q1755836}}の武器庫があったとする説が広まるが境内の史跡説明板（名古屋市教育委員会設置）による。{{Sfn|千種区の歴史|1981年|p=33}}{{Sfn|千種区史|1987年|p=428}}、平安末期成立の『{{jalink|尾張国内神名帳|Q11041830}}』では「高牟久天神」と書かれているため本来は「たかむく」と読み{{jalink|高向氏|Q109357793}}や{{jalink|高向神社|Q11668968}}に関係する可能性がある。 === 祭神 === * {{jalink|高皇産霊神}}（たかみむすびのかみ） * {{jalink|神皇産霊神}}（かみむすびのかみ） * {{jalink|応神天皇|Q317997}}（誉田別尊、ほんだわけのみこと） === 末社 === 境内には以下の末社がある。 {{Div col}} * 北野天神社 * 高牟龍神社 * 多賀社 * 金刀比羅社 * 厳島社 * 神明社 * 白山社 * 津島社 * 徳義稲荷社 * 祖霊社 * 秋葉社 {{Div col end}} === その他 === '''「恋の三社めぐり」'''として、{{jalink|山田天満宮|Q3571407}}（{{jalink|北区 (名古屋市)|Q1207964|北区}}山田町）・{{jalink|城山八幡宮|Q3482375}}（千種区{{jalink|城山町 (名古屋市)|Q24876539|城山町}}）とともに恋愛成就の{{jalink|巡礼|Q1644573}}スポットとなっている{{Cite web|和書|date= |url=http://www.nagoya-info.jp/shiru/koinosanshameguri.html |title=恋の三社めぐり（名古屋観光情報 名古屋コンシェルジュ） |publisher=（公財）名古屋観光コンベンションビューロー |accessdate=2016-03-18}}{{Cite web|和書|date= |url=http://www.shiroyama.or.jp/sansyameguri.htm |title=恋の三社めぐり |publisher=城山八幡宮 |accessdate=2016-03-18}}。もともと{{jalink|2002年|Q1987}}（{{jalink|平成|Q234510}}14年）に{{jalink|桜花学園高等学校|Q11538202|桜花学園高校}}のクラブ活動で企画され、{{jalink|2008年|Q2004}}（平成20年）に{{jalink|東海旅客鉄道|Q513679|JR東海}}がウオーキングイベントとして取り上げたことで広く知られるようになった。なお、当初は{{jalink|名古屋晴明神社|Q17229502}}（千種区{{jalink|清明山|Q17221162}}）・高牟神社・城山八幡宮の三社だった樋岡徹也「恋の三社めぐり　3神社巡り恋成就　桜花学園高生徒が企画、26日にイベント」、{{jalink|毎日新聞|Q1136866}}2007年5月1日付朝刊、地方版/愛知、20頁猪飼なつみ「あいちの考古楽　高牟神社　名古屋市千種区　わき水に病治癒伝説」、{{jalink|中日新聞|Q904678}}2008年12月13日付朝刊、市民版、19頁塩原賢「三社めぐりで恋ゲット？　高校生が企画、静かにブーム　名古屋・千種　」、{{jalink|朝日新聞|Q720503}}2012年2月3日付夕刊（名古屋本社版）、7頁{{Cite web|和書|url=http://opi-rina.chunichi.co.jp/tankentai/20100128_jinja.html |title=恋にまつわる3神社で恋愛成就祈願（オピ・探検隊） |publisher= 中日新聞Web |date=2010-01-28 |accessdate=2016-03-18}}。 境内には古い井戸があり、現在も{{jalink|手水|Q1069667}}に用いられている。別名の「古井八幡」や、鎮座地の旧町名「元古井町」・近隣地名の「古井ノ坂」（こいのさか）の由来ともなっている。 拝殿にかけられている社号額は、{{jalink|岸信介|Q315572}}の{{jalink|揮毫|Q11498466}}による。 === 所在地 === * {{jalink|愛知県|Q80434}}{{jalink|名古屋市|Q11751}}{{jalink|千種区|Q1207951}}{{jalink|今池 (名古屋市)|Q11377165|今池}}1-4-18 === アクセス === * {{jalink|東海旅客鉄道|Q513679|JR}}{{jalink|中央線 (名古屋地区)|Q11363767|中央本線}}・{{jalink|名古屋市営地下鉄|Q1144874}}{{jalink| 名古屋市営地下鉄東山線|Q1132799|東山線}}の{{jalink|千種駅|Q863068}}から南東に150m。 * 名古屋市営地下鉄東山線・名古屋市営地下鉄{{jalink|名古屋市営地下鉄桜通線|Q1132780|桜通線}}の{{jalink|今池駅 {{jalink|Gosha|Q134917316}} |Foundation = (tradition) Era of {{jalink|Emperor Seimu|Q316672}} |Main shrine = {{jalink|Ryuzo|Q210684}} |Alternative name = Furui Hachiman

Hachiman Shrine|Regular Festival = {{jalink|October 14th|Q2922}}-{{jalink|October 15th|Q2919}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = Nagoya }} [[File:Takamu shrine, Plaque of shrine name (Chikusa ward, Nagoya city. 2016.03.17).jpg|thumb|220px|right|The shrine name plaque on the shrine. At the bottom left is written ``Kishi Nobusuke.'' ]] [[File:Chikusa Takamu Shrine 2009-08B.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Torii of Tokugi Inari Shrine]] [[File:Takamu Shrine, Chōzuya (Chikusa ward, Nagoya city. 2016.03.02).JPG|thumb|220px|right|Chozuya at Takamu Shrine. Water is drawn from a well in the back and used for washing hands. ]] '''Takamu Shrine'' is located at {{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}{{jalink|Nagoya City|Q11751}}{{jalink|Chikusa Ward|Q1207951}}{{jalink|Imaike (Nagoya City)|Q11377165|Imaike}} {{jalink|Shrine|Q845945}} located in 1-chome. It is compared to {{jalink|Owari Province|Q514506}}{{jalink|Aichi District (Aichi Prefecture)|Q552687|Aichi District}} ``Takamu Shrine'' in ``{{jalink|Engishiki Shinmecho|Q11064932}}''. === History === According to the precincts information board, it is an old shrine that was founded in the era of {{jalink|Emperor Seimu|Q316672}}, and that {{jalink|Emperor Ojin|Q317997}} was enshrined in the era of {{jalink|Emperor Seiwa|Q349243}}. . Until {{jalink|early modern times|Q5308718}}, it was called '''Hachimangu''''. It was also called ``Funade Hachiman'' because there was a {{jalink|divine statue|Q11588751}} riding on a {{jalink|ship|Q1229765}}.After {{jalink|1441|Q6402}} ({{jalink|Kakichi|Q1193238}} first year), there are records of the construction and restoration of multiple shrines. The Shrines and Temples Edition includes the {{jalink|Kansho|Q1143928}} 2nd year ({{jalink|1461|Q6532}}) and {{jalink|astronomical studies that had been handed down at the time of compilation. (era name)|Q1192853|astronomical}} 1st year ({{jalink|1532|Q6436}}), {{jalink|Eiroku|Q1143341}}8th year ({{jalink|156 5th year|Q6634}}) and {{jalink|Keicho|Q1328185}}8th year ({{jalink|1603|Q6735}}) are listed (p.322-325). }}, both {{jalink|ridge tags|Q8008834}} say ``Koimura Hachiman Shrine'' and ``Hachiman Shrine.'' Before {{jalink|Keichō|Q1328185}}, it was located in Genkai Village, Hakusan, west of the current location (currently {{jalink|Naka Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1207921|Naka Ward}}{{jalink|Shinei|Q11502585}}, Hakusansha), but there is a legend that it was moved to its current location when {{jalink|Nagoya Castle|Q648629}} was being built over Kiyosu|Q11561274}}. "{{jalink|Memorandum of Kanbun Villages|Q137766629}}", which was established in the early Edo period, states, The Kojima family, who were {{jalink|Negi|Q11594490}} of Furui village, served as {{jalink|Shinto priest|Q15888735}} in {{jalink|early modern times|Q5308718}}. {{jalink|Owari Domain|Q1062072}} lord {{jalink|Tokugawa Mitsutomo|Q843245}} was {{jalink|Tenwa (Japan)|Q1145289|Tenwa}}There is a record that the shrine building was repaired in 1683|Q7651}} {{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1981|p=34}}{{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1987|p=429}}. Until {{jalink|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535}}, Gyokuryuzan Koshoin (still located to the east of our company) was {{jalink|Jinguji|Q1074971}}. In {{jalink|1873|Q7757}} (Meiji 5), it was ranked as {{jalink|gosha|Q134917316}} in the {{jalink|modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}. The shrine buildings were burnt down in the {{jalink|air raid|Q2380335}} of {{jalink|Pacific War|Q184425}}, but were rebuilt after the war.It was identified by {{jalink|Amano Nobukage|Q6535030}} as ``Takamu Shrine'' in Aichi District, Owari Province, which appears in the ``Engishiki Shinmyocho,'' and it became an established theory after the mid-Edo period. In {{jalink|1868|Q7717}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} first year), it was officially renamed "Takamu Shrine". However, the basis for comparing our company to Takamu Shrine is not clear, and {{jalink|Masao Tsuda|Q22123474}}, a {{jalink|local historian}} from the late Edo period, questions the idea of ​​comparing our company to ``Takamu Shrine.'' Takamu Shrines other than ours include Takamine Daimyojin (currently enshrined in {{jalink|Yagoto Shrine}}) and Takamu Shrine in {{jalink|Takanashi|Q50324950}}, but both of these shrines were built before {{jalink|medieval times|Q12554}} in {{jalink|Yamada District (Owari Province)|Q11470699|Yamada District}} Because it is within the area, it is not considered a likely candidate for Takamu Shrine in Aichi District. Masao Tsuda also lists {{jalink|Gokisho Hachimangu Shrine|Q3109957}} as another candidate for Takamu Shrine.From the {{jalink|Edo period}} onward, ``Takamu'' was nicknamed {{jalink|hoko}}, a symbol of {{jalink|weapons|Q728}}, and a theory spread that it was the site of the armory of the {{jalink|Mononobe clan|Q1755836}}, according to the historical site information board in the temple grounds (established by the Nagoya City Board of Education). {{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1981|p=33}}{{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1987|p=428}}, "{{jalink|Owari Domestic Shinto Name Book|Q11041830}}" established at the end of the Heian period. Since it is written as ``Takamuku Tenjin,'' it was originally read as ``Takamuku'' and may be related to {{jalink|Takamukai clan|Q109357793}} or {{jalink|Takamukai Shrine|Q11668968}}. === Enshrined God === * {{jalink|Takamimusubi no Kami}} * {{jalink|Kamimusubi no Kami}} * {{jalink|Emperor Ojin|Q317997}} (Honda Beszon, Hondawake no Mikoto) === Suesha === There are the following subsidiary shrines within the precincts. {{Div col}} * Kitano Tenjin Shrine * Takamuryu Shrine * Tagasha * Kotohirasha * Itsukushimasha * Shinmeisha * Hakusansha * Tsushimasha * Tokugi Inarisha *Soreisha Shrine * Akibasha {{Div col end}} === Others ===``Visiting three shrines of love'' includes {{jalink|Yamada Tenmangu|Q3571407}} ({{jalink|Kita Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1207964|Kita Ward}}Yamada Town), {{jalink|Shiroyama Hachimangu Shrine|Q3482375}} (Chikusa Ward{{jalink|Shiroyama Town) (Nagoya City|Q24876539|Shiroyama Town}}), it is a {{jalink|pilgrimage|Q1644573}} spot for fulfilling love. |publisher=Nagoya Tourism and Convention Bureau |accessdate=2016-03-18}}{{Cite web|Japanese book|date= |url=http://www.shiroyama.or.jp/sansyameguri.htm |title=Three shrines of love |publisher=Shiroyama Hachimangu Shrine |accessdate=2016-03-18}}. Originally planned in {{jalink|2002|Q1987}} ({{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}}14) as a club activity of {{jalink|Ohka Gakuen High School|Q11538202|Ohka Gakuen High School}} It became widely known when {{jalink|Central Railway Company|Q513679|JR Central}} featured it as a walking event in {{jalink|2008|Q2004}} (Heisei 20). In addition, there were originally three shrines: {{jalink|Nagoya Seimei Shrine|Q17229502}} (Chikusa Ward {{jalink|Seimeiyama|Q17221162}}), Takamu Shrine, and Shiroyama Hachiman Shrine. Guri Visiting 3 Shrines to Fulfill Love Planned by Ohka Gakuen High School Students, Event on 26th'', {{jalink|Mainichi Shimbun|Q1136866}}Morning edition, May 1, 2007, local edition/Aichi, page 20Natsumi Ikai "Aichi's Archaeological Music, Takamu Shrine, Chikusa Ward, Nagoya City, The Legend of Healing Illness in the Water in the Water", {{jalink|Chunichi Shimbun|Q904678}}Morning edition, citizen edition, December 13, 2008, p. 19Ken Shiobara, Sansha Get love by visiting? High school students plan, quietly booming in Chikusa, Nagoya”, {{jalink|Asahi Shimbun|Q720503}} Evening edition, February 3, 2012 (Nagoya headquarters edition), p. 7{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=http://opi-rina.chunichi.co.jp/tankentai/20100128_jinja.html |title=Pray for love fulfillment at three shrines related to love (Opi Exploration Team) |publisher= Chunichi Shimbun Web |date=2010-01-28 |accessdate=2016-03-18}}.There is an old well within the temple grounds, which is still used for {{jalink|washing hands|Q1069667}}. It is also the origin of its other name, ``Furui Hachiman,'' as well as the former name of the town where it is enshrined, ``Motofuruimachi,'' and the name of a nearby place, ``Koi no Saka.'' The plaque on the shrine's shrine is based on {{jalink|written|written|Q11498466}} by {{jalink|Nobusuke Kishi|Q315572}}. === Location === * {{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}{{jalink|Nagoya City|Q11751}}{{jalink|Chikusa Ward|Q1207951}}{{jalink|Imaike (Nagoya City)|Q11377165|Imaike}}1-4-18 === Access === * {{jalink|Central Railway|Q513679|JR}}{{jalink|Chuo Line (Nagoya area)|Q11363767|Chuo Main Line}}・{{jalink|Nagoya Municipal Subway|Q1144874}}{{jalink| 150m southeast of {{jalink|Chikusa Station|Q863068}} on the Nagoya Municipal Subway Higashiyama Shrine

{{jalink|Gosha|Q134917316}} |Foundation = (tradition) Era of {{jalink|Emperor Seimu|Q316672}} |Main shrine = {{jalink|Ryuzo|Q210684}} |Alternative name = Furui Hachiman
Funade Hachiman
Hachiman
Hachiman Shrine|Regular Festival = {{jalink|October 14th|Q2922}}-{{jalink|October 15th|Q2919}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = Nagoya }} [[File:Takamu shrine, Plaque of shrine name (Chikusa ward, Nagoya city. 2016.03.17).jpg|thumb|220px|right|The shrine name plaque on the shrine. At the bottom left is written ``Kishi Nobusuke.'' ]] [[File:Chikusa Takamu Shrine 2009-08B.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Torii of Tokugi Inari Shrine]] [[File:Takamu Shrine, Chōzuya (Chikusa ward, Nagoya city. 2016.03.02).JPG|thumb|220px|right|Chozuya at Takamu Shrine. Water is drawn from a well in the back and used for washing hands. ]] '''Takamu Shrine'' is located at {{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}{{jalink|Nagoya City|Q11751}}{{jalink|Chikusa Ward|Q1207951}}{{jalink|Imaike (Nagoya City)|Q11377165|Imaike}} {{jalink|Shrine|Q845945}} located in 1-chome. It is compared to {{jalink|Owari Province|Q514506}}{{jalink|Aichi District (Aichi Prefecture)|Q552687|Aichi District}} ``Takamu Shrine'' in ``{{jalink|Engishiki Shinmecho|Q11064932}}''. === History === According to the precincts information board, it is an old shrine that was founded in the era of {{jalink|Emperor Seimu|Q316672}}, and that {{jalink|Emperor Ojin|Q317997}} was enshrined in the era of {{jalink|Emperor Seiwa|Q349243}}. . Until {{jalink|early modern times|Q5308718}}, it was called '''Hachimangu''''. It was also called ``Funade Hachiman'' because there was a {{jalink|divine statue|Q11588751}} riding on a {{jalink|ship|Q1229765}}.After {{jalink|1441|Q6402}} ({{jalink|Kakichi|Q1193238}} first year), there are records of the construction and restoration of multiple shrines. The Shrines and Temples Edition includes the {{jalink|Kansho|Q1143928}} 2nd year ({{jalink|1461|Q6532}}) and {{jalink|astronomical studies that had been handed down at the time of compilation. (era name)|Q1192853|astronomical}} 1st year ({{jalink|1532|Q6436}}), {{jalink|Eiroku|Q1143341}}8th year ({{jalink|156 5th year|Q6634}}) and {{jalink|Keicho|Q1328185}}8th year ({{jalink|1603|Q6735}}) are listed (p.322-325). }}, both {{jalink|ridge tags|Q8008834}} say ``Koimura Hachiman Shrine'' and ``Hachiman Shrine.'' Before {{jalink|Keichō|Q1328185}}, it was located in Genkai Village, Hakusan, west of the current location (currently {{jalink|Naka Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1207921|Naka Ward}}{{jalink|Shinei|Q11502585}}, Hakusansha), but there is a legend that it was moved to its current location when {{jalink|Nagoya Castle|Q648629}} was being built over Kiyosu|Q11561274}}. "{{jalink|Memorandum of Kanbun Villages|Q137766629}}", which was established in the early Edo period, states, The Kojima family, who were {{jalink|Negi|Q11594490}} of Furui village, served as {{jalink|Shinto priest|Q15888735}} in {{jalink|early modern times|Q5308718}}. {{jalink|Owari Domain|Q1062072}} lord {{jalink|Tokugawa Mitsutomo|Q843245}} was {{jalink|Tenwa (Japan)|Q1145289|Tenwa}}There is a record that the shrine building was repaired in 1683|Q7651}} {{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1981|p=34}}{{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1987|p=429}}. Until {{jalink|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535}}, Gyokuryuzan Koshoin (still located to the east of our company) was {{jalink|Jinguji|Q1074971}}. In {{jalink|1873|Q7757}} (Meiji 5), it was ranked as {{jalink|gosha|Q134917316}} in the {{jalink|modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}. The shrine buildings were burnt down in the {{jalink|air raid|Q2380335}} of {{jalink|Pacific War|Q184425}}, but were rebuilt after the war.It was identified by {{jalink|Amano Nobukage|Q6535030}} as ``Takamu Shrine'' in Aichi District, Owari Province, which appears in the ``Engishiki Shinmyocho,'' and it became an established theory after the mid-Edo period. In {{jalink|1868|Q7717}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} first year), it was officially renamed "Takamu Shrine". However, the basis for comparing our company to Takamu Shrine is not clear, and {{jalink|Masao Tsuda|Q22123474}}, a {{jalink|local historian}} from the late Edo period, questions the idea of ​​comparing our company to ``Takamu Shrine.'' Takamu Shrines other than ours include Takamine Daimyojin (currently enshrined in {{jalink|Yagoto Shrine}}) and Takamu Shrine in {{jalink|Takanashi|Q50324950}}, but both of these shrines were built before {{jalink|medieval times|Q12554}} in {{jalink|Yamada District (Owari Province)|Q11470699|Yamada District}} Because it is within the area, it is not considered a likely candidate for Takamu Shrine in Aichi District. Masao Tsuda also lists {{jalink|Gokisho Hachimangu Shrine|Q3109957}} as another candidate for Takamu Shrine.From the {{jalink|Edo period}} onward, ``Takamu'' was nicknamed {{jalink|hoko}}, a symbol of {{jalink|weapons|Q728}}, and a theory spread that it was the site of the armory of the {{jalink|Mononobe clan|Q1755836}}, according to the historical site information board in the temple grounds (established by the Nagoya City Board of Education). {{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1981|p=33}}{{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1987|p=428}}, "{{jalink|Owari Domestic Shinto Name Book|Q11041830}}" established at the end of the Heian period. Since it is written as ``Takamuku Tenjin,'' it was originally read as ``Takamuku'' and may be related to {{jalink|Takamukai clan|Q109357793}} or {{jalink|Takamukai Shrine|Q11668968}}. === Enshrined God === * {{jalink|Takamimusubi no Kami}} * {{jalink|Kamimusubi no Kami}} * {{jalink|Emperor Ojin|Q317997}} (Honda Beszon, Hondawake no Mikoto) === Suesha === There are the following subsidiary shrines within the precincts. {{Div col}} * Kitano Tenjin Shrine * Takamuryu Shrine * Tagasha * Kotohirasha * Itsukushimasha * Shinmeisha * Hakusansha * Tsushimasha * Tokugi Inarisha *Soreisha Shrine * Akibasha {{Div col end}} === Others ===``Visiting three shrines of love'' includes {{jalink|Yamada Tenmangu|Q3571407}} ({{jalink|Kita Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1207964|Kita Ward}}Yamada Town), {{jalink|Shiroyama Hachimangu Shrine|Q3482375}} (Chikusa Ward{{jalink|Shiroyama Town) (Nagoya City|Q24876539|Shiroyama Town}}), it is a {{jalink|pilgrimage|Q1644573}} spot for fulfilling love. |publisher=Nagoya Tourism and Convention Bureau |accessdate=2016-03-18}}{{Cite web|Japanese book|date= |url=http://www.shiroyama.or.jp/sansyameguri.htm |title=Three shrines of love |publisher=Shiroyama Hachimangu Shrine |accessdate=2016-03-18}}. Originally planned in {{jalink|2002|Q1987}} ({{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}}14) as a club activity of {{jalink|Ohka Gakuen High School|Q11538202|Ohka Gakuen High School}} It became widely known when {{jalink|Central Railway Company|Q513679|JR Central}} featured it as a walking event in {{jalink|2008|Q2004}} (Heisei 20). In addition, there were originally three shrines: {{jalink|Nagoya Seimei Shrine|Q17229502}} (Chikusa Ward {{jalink|Seimeiyama|Q17221162}}), Takamu Shrine, and Shiroyama Hachiman Shrine. link|Q18233534}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Takamu Shrine |Image = [[File:Takamu shrine090809NI3.JPG|270px]]
Shrine
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |Location = {{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}{{jalink|Nagoya City|Q11751}}{{jalink|Chikusa Ward|Q1207951}}{{jalink|Imaike (Nagoya City)|Q11377165|Imaike}} 1-4-18 | Latitude degrees = 35 | Latitude minutes = 10 | Latitude seconds = 9.2 | N (northern latitude) and S (south latitude) = N | Longitude degrees = 136 | Longitude minutes = 55 | Longitude seconds = 57.1 | E(East longitude) and W(West longitude) = E | Map country code = JP-23 |Enshrined deity = {{jalink|Emperor Ojin|Q317997}} |Shakaku = {{jalink|Shikiuchisha}} (small)
{{jalink|Gosha|Q134917316}} |Foundation = (tradition) Era of {{jalink|Emperor Seimu|Q316672}} |Main shrine = {{jalink|Ryuzo|Q210684}} |Alternative name = Furui Hachiman
Funade Hachiman
Hachiman
Hachiman Shrine|Regular Festival = {{jalink|October 14th|Q2922}}-{{jalink|October 15th|Q2919}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = Nagoya }} [[File:Takamu shrine, Plaque of shrine name (Chikusa ward, Nagoya city. 2016.03.17).jpg|thumb|220px|right|The shrine name plaque on the shrine. At the bottom left is written ``Kishi Nobusuke.'' ]] [[File:Chikusa Takamu Shrine 2009-08B.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Torii of Tokugi Inari Shrine]] [[File:Takamu Shrine, Chōzuya (Chikusa ward, Nagoya city. 2016.03.02).JPG|thumb|220px|right|Chozuya at Takamu Shrine. Water is drawn from a well in the back and used for washing hands. ]] '''Takamu Shrine'' is located at {{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}{{jalink|Nagoya City|Q11751}}{{jalink|Chikusa Ward|Q1207951}}{{jalink|Imaike (Nagoya City)|Q11377165|Imaike}} {{jalink|Shrine|Q845945}} located in 1-chome. It is compared to {{jalink|Owari Province|Q514506}}{{jalink|Aichi District (Aichi Prefecture)|Q552687|Aichi District}} ``Takamu Shrine'' in ``{{jalink|Engishiki Shinmecho|Q11064932}}''. === History === According to the precincts information board, it is an old shrine that was founded in the era of {{jalink|Emperor Seimu|Q316672}}, and that {{jalink|Emperor Ojin|Q317997}} was enshrined in the era of {{jalink|Emperor Seiwa|Q349243}}. . Until {{jalink|early modern times|Q5308718}}, it was called '''Hachimangu''''. It was also called ``Funade Hachiman'' because there was a {{jalink|divine statue|Q11588751}} riding on a {{jalink|ship|Q1229765}}.After {{jalink|1441|Q6402}} ({{jalink|Kakichi|Q1193238}} first year), there are records of the construction and restoration of multiple shrines. The Shrines and Temples Edition includes the {{jalink|Kansho|Q1143928}} 2nd year ({{jalink|1461|Q6532}}) and {{jalink|astronomical studies that had been handed down at the time of compilation. (era name)|Q1192853|astronomical}} 1st year ({{jalink|1532|Q6436}}), {{jalink|Eiroku|Q1143341}}8th year ({{jalink|156 5th year|Q6634}}) and {{jalink|Keicho|Q1328185}}8th year ({{jalink|1603|Q6735}}) are listed (p.322-325). }}, both {{jalink|ridge tags|Q8008834}} say ``Koimura Hachiman Shrine'' and ``Hachiman Shrine.'' Before {{jalink|Keichō|Q1328185}}, it was located in Genkai Village, Hakusan, west of the current location (currently {{jalink|Naka Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1207921|Naka Ward}}{{jalink|Shinei|Q11502585}}, Hakusansha), but there is a legend that it was moved to its current location when {{jalink|Nagoya Castle|Q648629}} was being built over Kiyosu|Q11561274}}. "{{jalink|Memorandum of Kanbun Villages|Q137766629}}", which was established in the early Edo period, states, The Kojima family, who were {{jalink|Negi|Q11594490}} of Furui village, served as {{jalink|Shinto priest|Q15888735}} in {{jalink|early modern times|Q5308718}}. {{jalink|Owari Domain|Q1062072}} lord {{jalink|Tokugawa Mitsutomo|Q843245}} was {{jalink|Tenwa (Japan)|Q1145289|Tenwa}}There is a record that the shrine building was repaired in 1683|Q7651}} {{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1981|p=34}}{{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1987|p=429}}. Until {{jalink|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535}}, Gyokuryuzan Koshoin (still located to the east of our company) was {{jalink|Jinguji|Q1074971}}. In {{jalink|1873|Q7757}} (Meiji 5), it was ranked as {{jalink|gosha|Q134917316}} in the {{jalink|modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}. The shrine buildings were burnt down in the {{jalink|air raid|Q2380335}} of {{jalink|Pacific War|Q184425}}, but were rebuilt after the war.It was identified by {{jalink|Amano Nobukage|Q6535030}} as ``Takamu Shrine'' in Aichi District, Owari Province, which appears in the ``Engishiki Shinmyocho,'' and it became an established theory after the mid-Edo period. In {{jalink|1868|Q7717}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} first year), it was officially renamed "Takamu Shrine". However, the basis for comparing our company to Takamu Shrine is not clear, and {{jalink|Masao Tsuda|Q22123474}}, a {{jalink|local historian}} from the late Edo period, questions the idea of ​​comparing our company to ``Takamu Shrine.'' Takamu Shrines other than ours include Takamine Daimyojin (currently enshrined in {{jalink|Yagoto Shrine}}) and Takamu Shrine in {{jalink|Takanashi|Q50324950}}, but both of these shrines were built before {{jalink|medieval times|Q12554}} in {{jalink|Yamada District (Owari Province)|Q11470699|Yamada District}} Because it is within the area, it is not considered a likely candidate for Takamu Shrine in Aichi District. Masao Tsuda also lists {{jalink|Gokisho Hachimangu Shrine|Q3109957}} as another candidate for Takamu Shrine.From the {{jalink|Edo period}} onward, ``Takamu'' was nicknamed {{jalink|hoko}}, a symbol of {{jalink|weapons|Q728}}, and a theory spread that it was the site of the armory of the {{jalink|Mononobe clan|Q1755836}}, according to the historical site information board in the temple grounds (established by the Nagoya City Board of Education). {{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1981|p=33}}{{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1987|p=428}}, "{{jalink|Owari Domestic Shinto Name Book|Q11041830}}" established at the end of the Heian period. Since it is written as ``Takamuku Tenjin,'' it was originally read as ``Takamuku'' and may be related to {{jalink|Takamukai clan|Q109357793}} or {{jalink|Takamukai Shrine|Q11668968}}. === Enshrined God === * {{jalink|Takamimusubi no Kami}} * {{jalink|Kamimusubi no Kami}} * {{jalink|Emperor Ojin|Q317997}} (Honda Beszon, Hondawake no Mikoto) === Suesha === There are the following subsidiary shrines within the precincts. {{Div col}} * Kitano Tenjin Shrine * Takamuryu Shrine * Tagasha * Kotohirasha * Itsukushimasha * Shinmeisha * Hakusansha * Tsushimasha * Tokugi Inarisha *Soreisha Shrine * Akibasha {{Div col end}} === Others ===``Visiting three shrines of love'' includes {{jalink|Yamada Tenmangu|Q3571407}} ({{jalink|Kita Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1207964|Kita Ward}}Yamada Town), {{jalink|Shiroyama Hachimangu Shrine|Q3482375}} (Chikusa Ward{{jalink|Shiroyama Town) (Nagoya City|Q24876539|Shiroyama Town}}), it is a {{jalink|pilgrimage|Q1644573}} spot for fulfilling love. |publisher=Nagoya Tourism and Convention Bureau |accessdate=2016-03-18}}{{Cite web|Japanese book|date= |url=http://www.shiroyama.or.jp/sansyameguri.htm |title=Three shrines of love |publisher=Shiroyama Hachimangu Shrine |accessdate=2016-03-18}}. Originally planned in {{jalink|2002|Q1987}} ({{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}}14) as a club activity of {{jalink|Ohka Gakuen High School|Q11538202|Ohka Gakuen High School}} It became widely known when {{jalink|Central Railway Company|Q513679|JR Central}} featured it as a walking event in {{jalink|2008|Q2004}} (Heisei 20). In addition, there were originally three shrines: {{jalink|Nagoya Seimei Shrine|Q17229502}} (Chikusa Ward {{jalink|Seimeiyama|Q17221162}}), Takamu Shrine, and Shiroyama Hachiman Shrine. Guri Visiting 3 Shrines to Fulfill Love Planned by Ohka Gakuen High School Students, Event on 26th'', {{jalink|Mainichi Shimbun|Q1136866}}Morning edition, May 1, 2007, local edition/Aichi, page 20Natsumi Ikai "Aichi's Archaeological Music, Takamu Shrine, Chikusa Ward, Nagoya City, The Legend of Healing Illness in the Water in the Water", {{jalink|Chunichi Shimbun|Q904678}}Morning edition, citizen edition, December 13, 2008, p. 19Ken Shiobara, Sansha Get love by visiting? High school students plan, quietly booming in Chikusa, Nagoya”, {{jalink|Asahi Shimbun|Q720503}} Evening edition, February 3, 2012 (Nagoya headquarters edition), p. 7{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=http://opi-rina.chunichi.co.jp/tankentai/20100128_jinja.html |title=Pray for love fulfillment at three shrines related to love (Opi Exploration Team) |publisher= Chunichi Shimbun Web |date=2010-01-28 |accessdate=2016-03-18}}.There is an old well within the temple grounds, which is still used for {{jalink|washing hands|Q1069667}}. It is also the origin of its other name, ``Furui Hachiman,'' as well as the former name of the town where it is enshrined, ``Motofuruimachi,'' and the name of a nearby place, ``Koi no Saka.'' The plaque on the shrine's shrine is based on {{jalink|written|written|Q11498466}} by {{jalink|Nobusuke Kishi|Q315572}}. === Location === * {{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}{{jalink|Nagoya City|Q11751}}{{jalink|Chikusa Ward|Q1207951}}{{jalink|Imaike (Nagoya City)|Q11377165|Imaike}}1-4-18 === Access === * {{jalink|Central Railway|Q513679|JR}}{{jalink|Chuo Line (Nagoya area)|Q11363767|Chuo Main Line}}・{{jalink|Nagoya Municipal Subway|Q1144874}}{{jalink| 150m southeast of {{jalink|Chikusa Station|Q863068}} on the Nagoya Municipal Subway Higashiyama Line|Q1132799|Higashiyama Line}}. * 200m west of {{jalink|Imaike Station (Aichi Prefecture)|Q852173|Imaike Station}} of Nagoya Municipal Subway Higashiyama Line/Nagoya Municipal Subway {{jalink|Nagoya Hachiman Shrine|Regular Festival = {{jalink|October 14th|Q2922}}-{{jalink|October 15th|Q2919}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = Nagoya }} [[File:Takamu shrine, Plaque of shrine name (Chikusa ward, Nagoya city. 2016.03.17).jpg|thumb|220px|right|The shrine name plaque on the shrine. At the bottom left is written ``Kishi Nobusuke.'' ]] [[File:Chikusa Takamu Shrine 2009-08B.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Torii of Tokugi Inari Shrine]] [[File:Takamu Shrine, Chōzuya (Chikusa ward, Nagoya city. 2016.03.02).JPG|thumb|220px|right|Chozuya at Takamu Shrine. Water is drawn from a well in the back and used for washing hands. ]] '''Takamu Shrine'' is located at {{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}{{jalink|Nagoya City|Q11751}}{{jalink|Chikusa Ward|Q1207951}}{{jalink|Imaike (Nagoya City)|Q11377165|Imaike}} {{jalink|Shrine|Q845945}} located in 1-chome. It is compared to {{jalink|Owari Province|Q514506}}{{jalink|Aichi District (Aichi Prefecture)|Q552687|Aichi District}} ``Takamu Shrine'' in ``{{jalink|Engishiki Shinmecho|Q11064932}}''. === History === According to the precincts information board, it is an old shrine that was founded in the era of {{jalink|Emperor Seimu|Q316672}}, and that {{jalink|Emperor Ojin|Q317997}} was enshrined in the era of {{jalink|Emperor Seiwa|Q349243}}. . Until {{jalink|early modern times|Q5308718}}, it was called '''Hachimangu''''. It was also called ``Funade Hachiman'' because there was a {{jalink|divine statue|Q11588751}} riding on a {{jalink|ship|Q1229765}}.After {{jalink|1441|Q6402}} ({{jalink|Kakichi|Q1193238}} first year), there are records of the construction and restoration of multiple shrines. The Shrines and Temples Edition includes the {{jalink|Kansho|Q1143928}} 2nd year ({{jalink|1461|Q6532}}) and {{jalink|astronomical studies that had been handed down at the time of compilation. (era name)|Q1192853|astronomical}} 1st year ({{jalink|1532|Q6436}}), {{jalink|Eiroku|Q1143341}}8th year ({{jalink|156 5th year|Q6634}}) and {{jalink|Keicho|Q1328185}}8th year ({{jalink|1603|Q6735}}) are listed (p.322-325). }}, both {{jalink|ridge tags|Q8008834}} say ``Koimura Hachiman Shrine'' and ``Hachiman Shrine.'' Before {{jalink|Keichō|Q1328185}}, it was located in Genkai Village, Hakusan, west of the current location (currently {{jalink|Naka Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1207921|Naka Ward}}{{jalink|Shinei|Q11502585}}, Hakusansha), but there is a legend that it was moved to its current location when {{jalink|Nagoya Castle|Q648629}} was being built over Kiyosu|Q11561274}}. "{{jalink|Memorandum of Kanbun Villages|Q137766629}}", which was established in the early Edo period, states, The Kojima family, who were {{jalink|Negi|Q11594490}} of Furui village, served as {{jalink|Shinto priest|Q15888735}} in {{jalink|early modern times|Q5308718}}. {{jalink|Owari Domain|Q1062072}} lord {{jalink|Tokugawa Mitsutomo|Q843245}} was {{jalink|Tenwa (Japan)|Q1145289|Tenwa}}There is a record that the shrine building was repaired in 1683|Q7651}} {{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1981|p=34}}{{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1987|p=429}}. Until {{jalink|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535}}, Gyokuryuzan Koshoin (still located to the east of our company) was {{jalink|Jinguji|Q1074971}}. In {{jalink|1873|Q7757}} (Meiji 5), it was ranked as {{jalink|gosha|Q134917316}} in the {{jalink|modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}. The shrine buildings were burnt down in the {{jalink|air raid|Q2380335}} of {{jalink|Pacific War|Q184425}}, but were rebuilt after the war.It was identified by {{jalink|Amano Nobukage|Q6535030}} as ``Takamu Shrine'' in Aichi District, Owari Province, which appears in the ``Engishiki Shinmyocho,'' and it became an established theory after the mid-Edo period. In {{jalink|1868|Q7717}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} first year), it was officially renamed "Takamu Shrine". However, the basis for comparing our company to Takamu Shrine is not clear, and {{jalink|Masao Tsuda|Q22123474}}, a {{jalink|local historian}} from the late Edo period, questions the idea of ​​comparing our company to ``Takamu Shrine.'' Takamu Shrines other than ours include Takamine Daimyojin (currently enshrined in {{jalink|Yagoto Shrine}}) and Takamu Shrine in {{jalink|Takanashi|Q50324950}}, but both of these shrines were built before {{jalink|medieval times|Q12554}} in {{jalink|Yamada District (Owari Province)|Q11470699|Yamada District}} Because it is within the area, it is not considered a likely candidate for Takamu Shrine in Aichi District. Masao Tsuda also lists {{jalink|Gokisho Hachimangu Shrine|Q3109957}} as another candidate for Takamu Shrine.From the {{jalink|Edo period}} onward, ``Takamu'' was nicknamed {{jalink|hoko}}, a symbol of {{jalink|weapons|Q728}}, and a theory spread that it was the site of the armory of the {{jalink|Mononobe clan|Q1755836}}, according to the historical site information board in the temple grounds (established by the Nagoya City Board of Education). {{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1981|p=33}}{{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1987|p=428}}, "{{jalink|Owari Domestic Shinto Name Book|Q11041830}}" established at the end of the Heian period. Since it is written as ``Takamuku Tenjin,'' it was originally read as ``Takamuku'' and may be related to {{jalink|Takamukai clan|Q109357793}} or {{jalink|Takamukai Shrine|Q11668968}}. === Enshrined God === * {{jalink|Takamimusubi no Kami}} * {{jalink|Kamimusubi no Kami}} * {{jalink|Emperor Ojin|Q317997}} (Honda Beszon, Hondawake no Mikoto) === Suesha === There are the following subsidiary shrines within the precincts. {{Div col}} * Kitano Tenjin Shrine * Takamuryu Shrine * Tagasha * Kotohirasha * Itsukushimasha * Shinmeisha * Hakusansha * Tsushimasha * Tokugi Inarisha *Soreisha Shrine * Akibasha {{Div col end}} === Others ===``Visiting three shrines of love'' includes {{jalink|Yamada Tenmangu|Q3571407}} ({{jalink|Kita Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1207964|Kita Ward}}Yamada Town), {{jalink|Shiroyama Hachimangu Shrine|Q3482375}} (Chikusa Ward{{jalink|Shiroyama Town) (Nagoya City|Q24876539|Shiroyama Town}}), it is a {{jalink|pilgrimage|Q1644573}} spot for fulfilling love. |publisher=Nagoya Tourism and Convention Bureau |accessdate=2016-03-18}}{{Cite web|Japanese book|date= |url=http://www.shiroyama.or.jp/sansyameguri.htm |title=Three shrines of love |publisher=Shiroyama Hachimangu Shrine |accessdate=2016-03-18}}. Originally planned in {{jalink|2002|Q1987}} ({{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}}14) as a club activity of {{jalink|Ohka Gakuen High School|Q11538202|Ohka Gakuen High School}} It became widely known when {{jalink|Central Railway Company|Q513679|JR Central}} featured it as a walking event in {{jalink|2008|Q2004}} (Heisei 20). In addition, there were originally three shrines: {{jalink|Nagoya Seimei Shrine|Q17229502}} (Chikusa Ward {{jalink|Seimeiyama|Q17221162}}), Takamu Shrine, and Shiroyama Hachiman Shrine. Guri Visiting 3 Shrines to Fulfill Love Planned by Ohka Gakuen High School Students, Event on 26th'', {{jalink|Mainichi Shimbun|Q1136866}}Morning edition, May 1, 2007, local edition/Aichi, page 20Natsumi Ikai "Aichi's Archaeological Music, Takamu Shrine, Chikusa Ward, Nagoya City, The Legend of Healing Illness in the Water in the Water", {{jalink|Chunichi Shimbun|Q904678}}Morning edition, citizen edition, December 13, 2008, p. 19Ken Shiobara, Sansha Get love by visiting? High school students plan, quietly booming in Chikusa, Nagoya”, {{jalink|Asahi Shimbun|Q720503}} Evening edition, February 3, 2012 (Nagoya headquarters edition), p. 7{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=http://opi-rina.chunichi.co.jp/tankentai/20100128_jinja.html |title=Pray for love fulfillment at three shrines related to love (Opi Exploration Team) |publisher= Chunichi Shimbun Web |date=2010-01-28 |accessdate=2016-03-18}}.There is an old well within the temple grounds, which is still used for {{jalink|washing hands|Q1069667}}. It is also the origin of its other name, ``Furui Hachiman,'' as well as the former name of the town where it is enshrined, ``Motofuruimachi,'' and the name of a nearby place, ``Koi no Saka.'' The plaque on the shrine's shrine is based on {{jalink|written|written|Q11498466}} by {{jalink|Nobusuke Kishi|Q315572}}. === Location === * {{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}{{jalink|Nagoya Takamu Shrines other than ours include Takamine Daimyojin (currently enshrined in {{jalink|Yagoto Shrine}}) and Takamu Shrine in {{jalink|Takanashi|Q50324950}}, but both of these shrines were built before {{jalink|medieval times|Q12554}} in {{jalink|Yamada District (Owari Province)|Q11470699|Yamada District}} Because it is within the area, it is not considered a likely candidate for Takamu Shrine in Aichi District. Masao Tsuda also lists {{jalink|Gokisho Hachimangu Shrine|Q3109957}} as another candidate for Takamu Shrine.From the {{jalink|Edo period}} onward, ``Takamu'' was nicknamed {{jalink|hoko}}, a symbol of {{jalink|weapons|Q728}}, and a theory spread that it was the site of the armory of the {{jalink|Mononobe clan|Q1755836}}, according to the historical site information board in the temple grounds (established by the Nagoya City Board of Education). {{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1981|p=33}}{{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1987|p=428}}, "{{jalink|Owari Domestic Shinto Name Book|Q11041830}}" established at the end of the Heian period. Since it is written as ``Takamuku Tenjin,'' it was originally read as ``Takamuku'' and may be related to {{jalink|Takamukai clan|Q109357793}} or {{jalink|Takamukai Shrine|Q11668968}}. === Enshrined God === * {{jalink|Takamimusubi no Kami}} * {{jalink|Kamimusubi no Kami}} * {{jalink|Emperor Ojin|Q317997}} (Honda Beszon, Hondawake no Mikoto) === Suesha === There are the following subsidiary shrines within the precincts. {{Div col}} * Kitano Tenjin Shrine * Takamuryu Shrine * Tagasha * Kotohirasha * Itsukushimasha * Shinmeisha * Hakusansha * Tsushimasha * Tokugi Inarisha *Soreisha Shrine * Akibasha {{Div col end}} === Others ===``Visiting three shrines of love'' includes {{jalink|Yamada Tenmangu|Q3571407}} ({{jalink|Kita Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1207964|Kita Ward}}Yamada Town), {{jalink|Shiroyama Hachimangu Shrine|Q3482375}} (Chikusa Ward{{jalink|Shiroyama Town) (Nagoya City|Q24876539|Shiroyama Town}}), it is a {{jalink|pilgrimage|Q1644573}} spot for fulfilling love. |publisher=Nagoya Tourism and Convention Bureau |accessdate=2016-03-18}}{{Cite web|Japanese book|date= |url=http://www.shiroyama.or.jp/sansyameguri.htm |title=Three shrines of love |publisher=Shiroyama Hachimangu Shrine |accessdate=2016-03-18}}. Originally planned in {{jalink|2002|Q1987}} ({{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}}14) as a club activity of {{jalink|Ohka Gakuen High School|Q11538202|Ohka Gakuen High School}} It became widely known when {{jalink|Central Railway Company|Q513679|JR Central}} featured it as a walking event in {{jalink|2008|Q2004}} (Heisei 20). In addition, there were originally three shrines: {{jalink|Nagoya Seimei Shrine|Q17229502}} (Chikusa Ward {{jalink|Seimeiyama|Q17221162}}), Takamu Shrine, and Shiroyama Hachiman Shrine. Guri Visiting 3 Shrines to Fulfill Love Planned by Ohka Gakuen High School Students, Event on 26th'', {{jalink|Mainichi Shimbun|Q1136866}}Morning edition, May 1, 2007, local edition/Aichi, page 20Natsumi Ikai "Aichi's Archaeological Music, Takamu Shrine, Chikusa Ward, Nagoya City, The Legend of Healing Illness in the Water in the Water", {{jalink|Chunichi Shimbun|Q904678}}Morning edition, citizen edition, December 13, 2008, p. 19Ken Shiobara, Sansha Get love by visiting? High school students plan, quietly booming in Chikusa, Nagoya”, {{jalink|Asahi Shimbun|Q720503}} Evening edition, February 3, 2012 (Nagoya headquarters edition), p. 7{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=http://opi-rina.chunichi.co.jp/tankentai/20100128_jinja.html |title=Pray for love fulfillment at three shrines related to love (Opi Exploration Team) |publisher= Chunichi Shimbun Web |date=2010-01-28 |accessdate=2016-03-18}}.There is an old well within the temple grounds, which is still used for {{jalink|washing hands|Q1069667}}. It is also the origin of its other name, ``Furui Hachiman,'' as well as the former name of the town where it is enshrined, ``Motofuruimachi,'' and the name of a nearby place, ``Koi no Saka.''
