Water is drawn from a well in the back and used for washing hands. ]] '''Takamu Shrine'' is located at {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}{{ill|lt=Nagoya City|Q11751|WD=Q11751}}{{ill|lt=Chikusa Ward|Q1207951|WD=Q1207951}}{{ill|lt=Imaike (Nagoya City)|Q11377165|WD=Q11377165|lt=Imaike}} {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in 1-chome. It is compared to {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506}}{{ill|lt=Aichi District (Aichi Prefecture)|Q552687|WD=Q552687|lt=Aichi District}} ``Takamu Shrine'' in ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmecho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}''. === History === According to the precincts information board, it is an old shrine that was founded in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seimu|Q316672|WD=Q316672}}, and that {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}} was enshrined in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243}}. . Until {{ill|lt=early modern times|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718}}, it was called '''Hachimangu''''. It was also called ``Funade Hachiman'' because there was a {{ill|lt=divine statue|Q11588751|WD=Q11588751}} riding on a {{ill|lt=ship|Q1229765|WD=Q1229765}}.After {{ill|lt=1441|Q6402|WD=Q6402}} ({{ill|lt=Kakichi|Q1193238|WD=Q1193238}} first year), there are records of the construction and restoration of multiple shrines. The Shrines and Temples Edition includes the {{ill|lt=Kansho|Q1143928|WD=Q1143928}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1461|Q6532|WD=Q6532}}) and {{ill|lt=astronomical studies that had been handed down at the time of compilation. (era name)|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|lt=astronomical}} 1st year ({{ill|lt=1532|Q6436|WD=Q6436}}), {{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|WD=Q1143341}}8th year ({{ill|lt=156 5th year|Q6634|WD=Q6634}}) and {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}8th year ({{ill|lt=1603|Q6735|WD=Q6735}}) are listed (p.322-325). }}, both {{ill|lt=ridge tags|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834}} say ``Koimura Hachiman Shrine'' and ``Hachiman Shrine.'' Before {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}, it was located in Genkai Village, Hakusan, west of the current location (currently {{ill|lt=Naka Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1207921|WD=Q1207921|lt=Naka Ward}}{{ill|lt=Shinei|Q11502585|WD=Q11502585}}, Hakusansha), but there is a legend that it was moved to its current location when {{ill|lt=Nagoya Castle|Q648629|WD=Q648629}} was being built over Kiyosu|Q11561274}}. "{{ill|lt=Memorandum of Kanbun Villages|Q137766629|WD=Q137766629}}", which was established in the early Edo period, states, The Kojima family, who were {{ill|lt=Negi|Q11594490|WD=Q11594490}} of Furui village, served as {{ill|lt=Shinto priest|Q15888735|WD=Q15888735}} in {{ill|lt=early modern times|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718}}. {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072}} lord {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Mitsutomo|Q843245|WD=Q843245}} was {{ill|lt=Tenwa (Japan)|Q1145289|WD=Q1145289|lt=Tenwa}}There is a record that the shrine building was repaired in 1683|Q7651}} {{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1981|p=34}}{{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1987|p=429}}. Until {{ill|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535}}, Gyokuryuzan Koshoin (still located to the east of our company) was {{ill|lt=Jinguji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971}}. In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}} (Meiji 5), it was ranked as {{ill|lt=gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} in the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}. The shrine buildings were burnt down in the {{ill|lt=air raid|Q2380335|WD=Q2380335}} of {{ill|lt=Pacific War|Q184425|WD=Q184425}}, but were rebuilt after the war.It was identified by {{ill|lt=Amano Nobukage|Q6535030|WD=Q6535030}} as ``Takamu Shrine'' in Aichi District, Owari Province, which appears in the ``Engishiki Shinmyocho,'' and it became an established theory after the mid-Edo period. In {{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} first year), it was officially renamed "Takamu Shrine". Water is drawn from a well in the back and used for washing hands. ]] '''Takamu Shrine'' is located at {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}{{ill|lt=Nagoya City|Q11751|WD=Q11751}}{{ill|lt=Chikusa Ward|Q1207951|WD=Q1207951}}{{ill|lt=Imaike (Nagoya City)|Q11377165|WD=Q11377165|lt=Imaike}} {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in 1-chome. It is compared to {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506}}{{ill|lt=Aichi District (Aichi Prefecture)|Q552687|WD=Q552687|lt=Aichi District}} ``Takamu Shrine'' in ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmecho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}''. === History === According to the precincts information board, it is an old shrine that was founded in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seimu|Q316672|WD=Q316672}}, and that {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}} was enshrined in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243}}. . Until {{ill|lt=early modern times|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718}}, it was called '''Hachimangu''''. It was also called ``Funade Hachiman'' because there was a {{ill|lt=divine statue|Q11588751|WD=Q11588751}} riding on a {{ill|lt=ship|Q1229765|WD=Q1229765}}.After {{ill|lt=1441|Q6402|WD=Q6402}} ({{ill|lt=Kakichi|Q1193238|WD=Q1193238}} first year), there are records of the construction and restoration of multiple shrines. The Shrines and Temples Edition includes the {{ill|lt=Kansho|Q1143928|WD=Q1143928}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1461|Q6532|WD=Q6532}}) and {{ill|lt=astronomical studies that had been handed down at the time of compilation. (era name)|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|lt=astronomical}} 1st year ({{ill|lt=1532|Q6436|WD=Q6436}}), {{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|WD=Q1143341}}8th year ({{ill|lt=156 5th year|Q6634|WD=Q6634}}) and {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}8th year ({{ill|lt=1603|Q6735|WD=Q6735}}) are listed (p.322-325). }}, both {{ill|lt=ridge tags|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834}} say ``Koimura Hachiman Shrine'' and ``Hachiman Shrine.'' Before {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}, it was located in Genkai Village, Hakusan, west of the current location (currently {{ill|lt=Naka Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1207921|WD=Q1207921|lt=Naka Ward}}{{ill|lt=Shinei|Q11502585|WD=Q11502585}}, Hakusansha), but there is a legend that it was moved to its current location when {{ill|lt=Nagoya Castle|Q648629|WD=Q648629}} was being built over Kiyosu|Q11561274}}. "{{ill|lt=Memorandum of Kanbun Villages|Q137766629|WD=Q137766629}}", which was established in the early Edo period, states, The Kojima family, who were {{ill|lt=Negi|Q11594490|WD=Q11594490}} of Furui village, served as {{ill|lt=Shinto priest|Q15888735|WD=Q15888735}} in {{ill|lt=early modern times|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718}}. {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072}} lord {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Mitsutomo|Q843245|WD=Q843245}} was {{ill|lt=Tenwa (Japan)|Q1145289|WD=Q1145289|lt=Tenwa}}There is a record that the shrine building was repaired in 1683|Q7651}} {{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1981|p=34}}{{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1987|p=429}}. Until {{ill|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535}}, Gyokuryuzan Koshoin (still located to the east of our company) was {{ill|lt=Jinguji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971}}. In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}} (Meiji 5), it was ranked as {{ill|lt=gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} in the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}. The shrine buildings were burnt down in the {{ill|lt=air raid|Q2380335|WD=Q2380335}} of {{ill|lt=Pacific War|Q184425|WD=Q184425}}, but were rebuilt after the war.It was identified by {{ill|lt=Amano Nobukage|Q6535030|WD=Q6535030}} as ``Takamu Shrine'' in Aichi District, Owari Province, which appears in the ``Engishiki Shinmyocho,'' and it became an established theory after the mid-Edo period. In {{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} first year), it was officially renamed "Takamu Shrine". However, the basis for comparing our company to Takamu Shrine is not clear, and {{ill|lt=Masao Tsuda|Q22123474|WD=Q22123474}}, a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=local historian|UNKNOWN}} from the late Edo period, questions the idea of ​​comparing our company to ``Takamu Shrine.'' Takamu Shrines other than ours include Takamine Daimyojin (currently enshrined in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yagoto Shrine|UNKNOWN}}) and Takamu Shrine in {{ill|lt=Takanashi|Q50324950|WD=Q50324950}}, but both of these shrines were built before {{ill|lt=medieval times|Q12554|WD=Q12554}} in {{ill|lt=Yamada District (Owari Province)|Q11470699|WD=Q11470699|lt=Yamada District}} Because it is within the area, it is not considered a likely candidate for Takamu Shrine in Aichi District. Masao Tsuda also lists {{ill|lt=Gokisho Hachimangu Shrine|Q3109957|WD=Q3109957}} as another candidate for Takamu Shrine.From the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|UNKNOWN}} onward, ``Takamu'' was nicknamed {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=hoko|UNKNOWN}}, a symbol of {{ill|lt=weapons|Q728|WD=Q728}}, and a theory spread that it was the site of the armory of the {{ill|lt=Mononobe clan|Q1755836|WD=Q1755836}}, according to the historical site information board in the temple grounds (established by the Nagoya City Board of Education). {{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1981|p=33}}{{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1987|p=428}}, "{{ill|lt=Owari Domestic Shinto Name Book|Q11041830|WD=Q11041830}}" established at the end of the Heian period. Since it is written as ``Takamuku Tenjin,'' it was originally read as ``Takamuku'' and may be related to {{ill|lt=Takamukai clan|Q109357793|WD=Q109357793}} or {{ill|lt=Takamukai Shrine|Q11668968|WD=Q11668968}}. === Enshrined God === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takamimusubi no Kami|UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamimusubi no Kami|UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}} (Honda Beszon, Hondawake no Mikoto) === Suesha === There are the following subsidiary shrines within the precincts. {{Div col}} * Kitano Tenjin Shrine * Takamuryu Shrine * Tagasha * Kotohirasha * Itsukushimasha * Shinmeisha * Hakusansha * Tsushimasha * Tokugi Inarisha *Soreisha Shrine * Akibasha {{Div col end}} === Others ===``Visiting three shrines of love'' includes {{ill|lt=Yamada Tenmangu|Q3571407|WD=Q3571407}} ({{ill|lt=Kita Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1207964|WD=Q1207964|lt=Kita Ward}}Yamada Town), {{ill|lt=Shiroyama Hachimangu Shrine|Q3482375|WD=Q3482375}} (Chikusa Ward{{ill|lt=Shiroyama Town) (Nagoya City|Q24876539|WD=Q24876539|lt=Shiroyama Town}}), it is a {{ill|lt=pilgrimage|Q1644573|WD=Q1644573}} spot for fulfilling love. |publisher=Nagoya Tourism and Convention Bureau |accessdate=2016-03-18}}{{Cite web|Japanese book|date= |url=http://www.shiroyama.or.jp/sansyameguri.htm |title=Three shrines of love |publisher=Shiroyama Hachimangu Shrine |accessdate=2016-03-18}}. Originally planned in {{ill|lt=2002|Q1987|WD=Q1987}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}}14) as a club activity of {{ill|lt=Ohka Gakuen High School|Q11538202|WD=Q11538202|lt=Ohka Gakuen High School}} It became widely known when {{ill|lt=Central Railway Company|Q513679|WD=Q513679|lt=JR Central}} featured it as a walking event in {{ill|lt=2008|Q2004|WD=Q2004}} (Heisei 20). In addition, there were originally three shrines: {{ill|lt=Nagoya Seimei Shrine|Q17229502|WD=Q17229502}} (Chikusa Ward {{ill|lt=Seimeiyama|Q17221162|WD=Q17221162}}), Takamu Shrine, and Shiroyama Hachiman Shrine. Guri Visiting 3 Shrines to Fulfill Love Planned by Ohka Gakuen High School Students, Event on 26th'', {{ill|lt=Mainichi Shimbun|Q1136866|WD=Q1136866}}Morning edition, May 1, 2007, local edition/Aichi, page 20Natsumi Ikai "Aichi's Archaeological Music, Takamu Shrine, Chikusa Ward, Nagoya City, The Legend of Healing Illness in the Water in the Water", {{ill|lt=Chunichi Shimbun|Q904678|WD=Q904678}}Morning edition, citizen edition, December 13, 2008, p. 19Ken Shiobara, Sansha Get love by visiting? High school students plan, quietly booming in Chikusa, Nagoya”, {{ill|lt=Asahi Shimbun|Q720503|WD=Q720503}} Evening edition, February 3, 2012 (Nagoya headquarters edition), p. 7{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=http://opi-rina.chunichi.co.jp/tankentai/20100128_jinja.html |title=Pray for love fulfillment at three shrines related to love (Opi Exploration Team) |publisher= Chunichi Shimbun Web |date=2010-01-28 |accessdate=2016-03-18}}.There is an old well within the temple grounds, which is still used for {{ill|lt=washing hands|Q1069667|WD=Q1069667}}. It is also the origin of its other name, ``Furui Hachiman,'' as well as the former name of the town where it is enshrined, ``Motofuruimachi,'' and the name of a nearby place, ``Koi no Saka.'' Hachiman Shrine|Regular Festival = {{ill|lt=October 14th|Q2922|WD=Q2922}}-{{ill|lt=October 15th|Q2919|WD=Q2919}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = Nagoya }} [[File:Takamu shrine, Plaque of shrine name (Chikusa ward, Nagoya city. 2016.03.17).jpg|thumb|220px|right|The shrine name plaque on the shrine. At the bottom left is written ``Kishi Nobusuke.'' ]] [[File:Chikusa Takamu Shrine 2009-08B.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Torii of Tokugi Inari Shrine]] [[File:Takamu Shrine, Chōzuya (Chikusa ward, Nagoya city. 2016.03.02).JPG|thumb|220px|right|Chozuya at Takamu Shrine. Water is drawn from a well in the back and used for washing hands. ]] '''Takamu Shrine'' is located at {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}{{ill|lt=Nagoya City|Q11751|WD=Q11751}}{{ill|lt=Chikusa Ward|Q1207951|WD=Q1207951}}{{ill|lt=Imaike (Nagoya City)|Q11377165|WD=Q11377165|lt=Imaike}} {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in 1-chome. It is compared to {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506}}{{ill|lt=Aichi District (Aichi Prefecture)|Q552687|WD=Q552687|lt=Aichi District}} ``Takamu Shrine'' in ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmecho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}''. === History === According to the precincts information board, it is an old shrine that was founded in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seimu|Q316672|WD=Q316672}}, and that {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}} was enshrined in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243}}. . Until {{ill|lt=early modern times|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718}}, it was called '''Hachimangu''''. It was also called ``Funade Hachiman'' because there was a {{ill|lt=divine statue|Q11588751|WD=Q11588751}} riding on a {{ill|lt=ship|Q1229765|WD=Q1229765}}.After {{ill|lt=1441|Q6402|WD=Q6402}} ({{ill|lt=Kakichi|Q1193238|WD=Q1193238}} first year), there are records of the construction and restoration of multiple shrines. The Shrines and Temples Edition includes the {{ill|lt=Kansho|Q1143928|WD=Q1143928}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1461|Q6532|WD=Q6532}}) and {{ill|lt=astronomical studies that had been handed down at the time of compilation. (era name)|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|lt=astronomical}} 1st year ({{ill|lt=1532|Q6436|WD=Q6436}}), {{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|WD=Q1143341}}8th year ({{ill|lt=156 5th year|Q6634|WD=Q6634}}) and {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}8th year ({{ill|lt=1603|Q6735|WD=Q6735}}) are listed (p.322-325). }}, both {{ill|lt=ridge tags|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834}} say ``Koimura Hachiman Shrine'' and ``Hachiman Shrine.'' Before {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}, it was located in Genkai Village, Hakusan, west of the current location (currently {{ill|lt=Naka Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1207921|WD=Q1207921|lt=Naka Ward}}{{ill|lt=Shinei|Q11502585|WD=Q11502585}}, Hakusansha), but there is a legend that it was moved to its current location when {{ill|lt=Nagoya Castle|Q648629|WD=Q648629}} was being built over Kiyosu|Q11561274}}. "{{ill|lt=Memorandum of Kanbun Villages|Q137766629|WD=Q137766629}}", which was established in the early Edo period, states, The Kojima family, who were {{ill|lt=Negi|Q11594490|WD=Q11594490}} of Furui village, served as {{ill|lt=Shinto priest|Q15888735|WD=Q15888735}} in {{ill|lt=early modern times|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718}}. {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072}} lord {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Mitsutomo|Q843245|WD=Q843245}} was {{ill|lt=Tenwa (Japan)|Q1145289|WD=Q1145289|lt=Tenwa}}There is a record that the shrine building was repaired in 1683|Q7651}} {{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1981|p=34}}{{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1987|p=429}}. Until {{ill|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535}}, Gyokuryuzan Koshoin (still located to the east of our company) was {{ill|lt=Jinguji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971}}. In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}} (Meiji 5), it was ranked as {{ill|lt=gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} in the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}. The shrine buildings were burnt down in the {{ill|lt=air raid|Q2380335|WD=Q2380335}} of {{ill|lt=Pacific War|Q184425|WD=Q184425}}, but were rebuilt after the war.It was identified by {{ill|lt=Amano Nobukage|Q6535030|WD=Q6535030}} as ``Takamu Shrine'' in Aichi District, Owari Province, which appears in the ``Engishiki Shinmyocho,'' and it became an established theory after the mid-Edo period. In {{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} first year), it was officially renamed "Takamu Shrine". However, the basis for comparing our company to Takamu Shrine is not clear, and {{ill|lt=Masao Tsuda|Q22123474|WD=Q22123474}}, a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=local historian|1=UNKNOWN}} from the late Edo period, questions the idea of ​​comparing our company to ``Takamu Shrine.'' Takamu Shrines other than ours include Takamine Daimyojin (currently enshrined in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yagoto Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}) and Takamu Shrine in {{ill|lt=Takanashi|Q50324950|WD=Q50324950}}, but both of these shrines were built before {{ill|lt=medieval times|Q12554|WD=Q12554}} in {{ill|lt=Yamada District (Owari Province)|Q11470699|WD=Q11470699|lt=Yamada District}} Because it is within the area, it is not considered a likely candidate for Takamu Shrine in Aichi District. Masao Tsuda also lists {{ill|lt=Gokisho Hachimangu Shrine|Q3109957|WD=Q3109957}} as another candidate for Takamu Shrine.From the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|1=UNKNOWN}} onward, ``Takamu'' was nicknamed {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=hoko|1=UNKNOWN}}, a symbol of {{ill|lt=weapons|Q728|WD=Q728}}, and a theory spread that it was the site of the armory of the {{ill|lt=Mononobe clan|Q1755836|WD=Q1755836}}, according to the historical site information board in the temple grounds (established by the Nagoya City Board of Education). {{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1981|p=33}}{{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1987|p=428}}, "{{ill|lt=Owari Domestic Shinto Name Book|Q11041830|WD=Q11041830}}" established at the end of the Heian period. Since it is written as ``Takamuku Tenjin,'' it was originally read as ``Takamuku'' and may be related to {{ill|lt=Takamukai clan|Q109357793|WD=Q109357793}} or {{ill|lt=Takamukai Shrine|Q11668968|WD=Q11668968}}. === Enshrined God === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takamimusubi no Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamimusubi no Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}} (Honda Beszon, Hondawake no Mikoto) === Suesha === There are the following subsidiary shrines within the precincts. {{Div col}} * Kitano Tenjin Shrine * Takamuryu Shrine * Tagasha * Kotohirasha * Itsukushimasha * Shinmeisha * Hakusansha * Tsushimasha * Tokugi Inarisha *Soreisha Shrine * Akibasha {{Div col end}} === Others ===``Visiting three shrines of love'' includes {{ill|lt=Yamada Tenmangu|Q3571407|WD=Q3571407}} ({{ill|lt=Kita Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1207964|WD=Q1207964|lt=Kita Ward}}Yamada Town), {{ill|lt=Shiroyama Hachimangu Shrine|Q3482375|WD=Q3482375}} (Chikusa Ward{{ill|lt=Shiroyama Town) (Nagoya City|Q24876539|WD=Q24876539|lt=Shiroyama Town}}), it is a {{ill|lt=pilgrimage|Q1644573|WD=Q1644573}} spot for fulfilling love. |publisher=Nagoya Tourism and Convention Bureau |accessdate=2016-03-18}}{{Cite web|Japanese book|date= |url=http://www.shiroyama.or.jp/sansyameguri.htm |title=Three shrines of love |publisher=Shiroyama Hachimangu Shrine |accessdate=2016-03-18}}. Originally planned in {{ill|lt=2002|Q1987|WD=Q1987}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}}14) as a club activity of {{ill|lt=Ohka Gakuen High School|Q11538202|WD=Q11538202|lt=Ohka Gakuen High School}} It became widely known when {{ill|lt=Central Railway Company|Q513679|WD=Q513679|lt=JR Central}} featured it as a walking event in {{ill|lt=2008|Q2004|WD=Q2004}} (Heisei 20). In addition, there were originally three shrines: {{ill|lt=Nagoya Seimei Shrine|Q17229502|WD=Q17229502}} (Chikusa Ward {{ill|lt=Seimeiyama|Q17221162|WD=Q17221162}}), Takamu Shrine, and Shiroyama Hachiman Shrine. Guri Visiting 3 Shrines to Fulfill Love Planned by Ohka Gakuen High School Students, Event on 26th'', {{ill|lt=Mainichi Shimbun|Q1136866|WD=Q1136866}}Morning edition, May 1, 2007, local edition/Aichi, page 20Natsumi Ikai "Aichi's Archaeological Music, Takamu Shrine, Chikusa Ward, Nagoya City, The Legend of Healing Illness in the Water in the Water", {{ill|lt=Chunichi Shimbun|Q904678|WD=Q904678}}Morning edition, citizen edition, December 13, 2008, p. 19Ken Shiobara, Sansha Get love by visiting? High school students plan, quietly booming in Chikusa, Nagoya”, {{ill|lt=Asahi Shimbun|Q720503|WD=Q720503}} Evening edition, February 3, 2012 (Nagoya headquarters edition), p. 7{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=http://opi-rina.chunichi.co.jp/tankentai/20100128_jinja.html |title=Pray for love fulfillment at three shrines related to love (Opi Exploration Team) |publisher= Chunichi Shimbun Web |date=2010-01-28 |accessdate=2016-03-18}}.There is an old well within the temple grounds, which is still used for {{ill|lt=washing hands|Q1069667|WD=Q1069667}}. It is also the origin of its other name, ``Furui Hachiman,'' as well as the former name of the town where it is enshrined, ``Motofuruimachi,'' and the name of a nearby place, ``Koi no Saka.'' The plaque on the shrine's shrine is based on {{ill|lt=written|written|WD=written|lt=Q11498466}} by {{ill|lt=Nobusuke Kishi|Q315572|WD=Q315572}}. === Location === * {{ill|lt=Aichi Hachiman Shrine|Regular Festival = {{ill|lt=October 14th|Q2922|WD=Q2922}}-{{ill|lt=October 15th|Q2919|WD=Q2919}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = Nagoya }} [[File:Takamu shrine, Plaque of shrine name (Chikusa ward, Nagoya city. 2016.03.17).jpg|thumb|220px|right|The shrine name plaque on the shrine. At the bottom left is written ``Kishi Nobusuke.'' ]] [[File:Chikusa Takamu Shrine 2009-08B.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Torii of Tokugi Inari Shrine]] [[File:Takamu Shrine, Chōzuya (Chikusa ward, Nagoya city. 2016.03.02).JPG|thumb|220px|right|Chozuya at Takamu Shrine. Water is drawn from a well in the back and used for washing hands. ]] '''Takamu Shrine'' is located at {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}{{ill|lt=Nagoya City|Q11751|WD=Q11751}}{{ill|lt=Chikusa Ward|Q1207951|WD=Q1207951}}{{ill|lt=Imaike (Nagoya City)|Q11377165|WD=Q11377165|lt=Imaike}} {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in 1-chome. It is compared to {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506}}{{ill|lt=Aichi District (Aichi Prefecture)|Q552687|WD=Q552687|lt=Aichi District}} ``Takamu Shrine'' in ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmecho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}''. === History === According to the precincts information board, it is an old shrine that was founded in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seimu|Q316672|WD=Q316672}}, and that {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}} was enshrined in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243}}. . Until {{ill|lt=early modern times|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718}}, it was called '''Hachimangu''''. It was also called ``Funade Hachiman'' because there was a {{ill|lt=divine statue|Q11588751|WD=Q11588751}} riding on a {{ill|lt=ship|Q1229765|WD=Q1229765}}.After {{ill|lt=1441|Q6402|WD=Q6402}} ({{ill|lt=Kakichi|Q1193238|WD=Q1193238}} first year), there are records of the construction and restoration of multiple shrines. The Shrines and Temples Edition includes the {{ill|lt=Kansho|Q1143928|WD=Q1143928}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1461|Q6532|WD=Q6532}}) and {{ill|lt=astronomical studies that had been handed down at the time of compilation. (era name)|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|lt=astronomical}} 1st year ({{ill|lt=1532|Q6436|WD=Q6436}}), {{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|WD=Q1143341}}8th year ({{ill|lt=156 5th year|Q6634|WD=Q6634}}) and {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}8th year ({{ill|lt=1603|Q6735|WD=Q6735}}) are listed (p.322-325). }}, both {{ill|lt=ridge tags|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834}} say ``Koimura Hachiman Shrine'' and ``Hachiman Shrine.'' Before {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}, it was located in Genkai Village, Hakusan, west of the current location (currently {{ill|lt=Naka Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1207921|WD=Q1207921|lt=Naka Ward}}{{ill|lt=Shinei|Q11502585|WD=Q11502585}}, Hakusansha), but there is a legend that it was moved to its current location when {{ill|lt=Nagoya Castle|Q648629|WD=Q648629}} was being built over Kiyosu|Q11561274}}. "{{ill|lt=Memorandum of Kanbun Villages|Q137766629|WD=Q137766629}}", which was established in the early Edo period, states, The Kojima family, who were {{ill|lt=Negi|Q11594490|WD=Q11594490}} of Furui village, served as {{ill|lt=Shinto priest|Q15888735|WD=Q15888735}} in {{ill|lt=early modern times|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718}}. {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072}} lord {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Mitsutomo|Q843245|WD=Q843245}} was {{ill|lt=Tenwa (Japan)|Q1145289|WD=Q1145289|lt=Tenwa}}There is a record that the shrine building was repaired in 1683|Q7651}} {{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1981|p=34}}{{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1987|p=429}}. Until {{ill|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535}}, Gyokuryuzan Koshoin (still located to the east of our company) was {{ill|lt=Jinguji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971}}. In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}} (Meiji 5), it was ranked as {{ill|lt=gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} in the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}. The shrine buildings were burnt down in the {{ill|lt=air raid|Q2380335|WD=Q2380335}} of {{ill|lt=Pacific War|Q184425|WD=Q184425}}, but were rebuilt after the war.It was identified by {{ill|lt=Amano Nobukage|Q6535030|WD=Q6535030}} as ``Takamu Shrine'' in Aichi District, Owari Province, which appears in the ``Engishiki Shinmyocho,'' and it became an established theory after the mid-Edo period. In {{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} first year), it was officially renamed "Takamu Shrine". However, the basis for comparing our company to Takamu Shrine is not clear, and {{ill|lt=Masao Tsuda|Q22123474|WD=Q22123474}}, a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=local historian|1=UNKNOWN}} from the late Edo period, questions the idea of ​​comparing our company to ``Takamu Shrine.'' Takamu Shrines other than ours include Takamine Daimyojin (currently enshrined in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yagoto Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}) and Takamu Shrine in {{ill|lt=Takanashi|Q50324950|WD=Q50324950}}, but both of these shrines were built before {{ill|lt=medieval times|Q12554|WD=Q12554}} in {{ill|lt=Yamada District (Owari Province)|Q11470699|WD=Q11470699|lt=Yamada District}} Because it is within the area, it is not considered a likely candidate for Takamu Shrine in Aichi District. Masao Tsuda also lists {{ill|lt=Gokisho Hachimangu Shrine|Q3109957|WD=Q3109957}} as another candidate for Takamu Shrine.From the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|1=UNKNOWN}} onward, ``Takamu'' was nicknamed {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=hoko|1=UNKNOWN}}, a symbol of {{ill|lt=weapons|Q728|WD=Q728}}, and a theory spread that it was the site of the armory of the {{ill|lt=Mononobe clan|Q1755836|WD=Q1755836}}, according to the historical site information board in the temple grounds (established by the Nagoya City Board of Education). {{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1981|p=33}}{{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1987|p=428}}, "{{ill|lt=Owari Domestic Shinto Name Book|Q11041830|WD=Q11041830}}" established at the end of the Heian period. Since it is written as ``Takamuku Tenjin,'' it was originally read as ``Takamuku'' and may be related to {{ill|lt=Takamukai clan|Q109357793|WD=Q109357793}} or {{ill|lt=Takamukai Shrine|Q11668968|WD=Q11668968}}. === Enshrined God === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takamimusubi no Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamimusubi no Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}} (Honda Beszon, Hondawake no Mikoto) === Suesha === There are the following subsidiary shrines within the precincts. {{Div col}} * Kitano Tenjin Shrine * Takamuryu Shrine * Tagasha * Kotohirasha * Itsukushimasha * Shinmeisha * Hakusansha * Tsushimasha * Tokugi Inarisha *Soreisha Shrine * Akibasha {{Div col end}} === Others ===``Visiting three shrines of love'' includes {{ill|lt=Yamada Tenmangu|Q3571407|WD=Q3571407}} ({{ill|lt=Kita Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1207964|WD=Q1207964|lt=Kita Ward}}Yamada Town), {{ill|lt=Shiroyama Hachimangu Shrine|Q3482375|WD=Q3482375}} (Chikusa Ward{{ill|lt=Shiroyama Town) (Nagoya City|Q24876539|WD=Q24876539|lt=Shiroyama Town}}), it is a {{ill|lt=pilgrimage|Q1644573|WD=Q1644573}} spot for fulfilling love. |publisher=Nagoya Tourism and Convention Bureau |accessdate=2016-03-18}}{{Cite web|Japanese book|date= |url=http://www.shiroyama.or.jp/sansyameguri.htm |title=Three shrines of love |publisher=Shiroyama Hachimangu Shrine |accessdate=2016-03-18}}. Originally planned in {{ill|lt=2002|Q1987|WD=Q1987}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}}14) as a club activity of {{ill|lt=Ohka Gakuen High School|Q11538202|WD=Q11538202|lt=Ohka Gakuen High School}} It became widely known when {{ill|lt=Central Railway Company|Q513679|WD=Q513679|lt=JR Central}} featured it as a walking event in {{ill|lt=2008|Q2004|WD=Q2004}} (Heisei 20). In addition, there were originally three shrines: {{ill|lt=Nagoya Seimei Shrine|Q17229502|WD=Q17229502}} (Chikusa Ward {{ill|lt=Seimeiyama|Q17221162|WD=Q17221162}}), Takamu Shrine, and Shiroyama Hachiman Shrine. Guri Visiting 3 Shrines to Fulfill Love Planned by Ohka Gakuen High School Students, Event on 26th'', {{ill|lt=Mainichi Shimbun|Q1136866|WD=Q1136866}}Morning edition, May 1, 2007, local edition/Aichi, page 20Natsumi Ikai "Aichi's Archaeological Music, Takamu Shrine, Chikusa Ward, Nagoya City, The Legend of Healing Illness in the Water in the Water", {{ill|lt=Chunichi Shimbun|Q904678|WD=Q904678}}Morning edition, citizen edition, December 13, 2008, p. 19Ken Shiobara, Sansha Get love by visiting? High school students plan, quietly booming in Chikusa, Nagoya”, {{ill|lt=Asahi Shimbun|Q720503|WD=Q720503}} Evening edition, February 3, 2012 (Nagoya headquarters edition), p. 7{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=http://opi-rina.chunichi.co.jp/tankentai/20100128_jinja.html |title=Pray for love fulfillment at three shrines related to love (Opi Exploration Team) |publisher= Chunichi Shimbun Web |date=2010-01-28 |accessdate=2016-03-18}}.There is an old well within the temple grounds, which is still used for {{ill|lt=washing hands|Q1069667|WD=Q1069667}}. It is also the origin of its other name, ``Furui Hachiman,'' as well as the former name of the town where it is enshrined, ``Motofuruimachi,'' and the name of a nearby place, ``Koi no Saka.'' The plaque on the shrine's shrine is based on {{ill|lt=written|written|WD=written|lt=Q11498466}} by {{ill|lt=Nobusuke Kishi|Q315572|WD=Q315572}}. === Location === * {{ill|lt=Aichi At the bottom left is written ``Kishi Nobusuke.'' ]] [[File:Chikusa Takamu Shrine 2009-08B.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Torii of Tokugi Inari Shrine]] [[File:Takamu Shrine, Chōzuya (Chikusa ward, Nagoya city. 2016.03.02).JPG|thumb|220px|right|Chozuya at Takamu Shrine. Water is drawn from a well in the back and used for washing hands. ]] '''Takamu Shrine'' is located at {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}{{ill|lt=Nagoya City|Q11751|WD=Q11751}}{{ill|lt=Chikusa Ward|Q1207951|WD=Q1207951}}{{ill|lt=Imaike (Nagoya City)|Q11377165|WD=Q11377165|lt=Imaike}} {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in 1-chome. It is compared to {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506}}{{ill|lt=Aichi District (Aichi Prefecture)|Q552687|WD=Q552687|lt=Aichi District}} ``Takamu Shrine'' in ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmecho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}''. === History === According to the precincts information board, it is an old shrine that was founded in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seimu|Q316672|WD=Q316672}}, and that {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}} was enshrined in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243}}. . Until {{ill|lt=early modern times|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718}}, it was called '''Hachimangu''''. It was also called ``Funade Hachiman'' because there was a {{ill|lt=divine statue|Q11588751|WD=Q11588751}} riding on a {{ill|lt=ship|Q1229765|WD=Q1229765}}.After {{ill|lt=1441|Q6402|WD=Q6402}} ({{ill|lt=Kakichi|Q1193238|WD=Q1193238}} first year), there are records of the construction and restoration of multiple shrines. The Shrines and Temples Edition includes the {{ill|lt=Kansho|Q1143928|WD=Q1143928}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1461|Q6532|WD=Q6532}}) and {{ill|lt=astronomical studies that had been handed down at the time of compilation. (era name)|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|lt=astronomical}} 1st year ({{ill|lt=1532|Q6436|WD=Q6436}}), {{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|WD=Q1143341}}8th year ({{ill|lt=156 5th year|Q6634|WD=Q6634}}) and {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}8th year ({{ill|lt=1603|Q6735|WD=Q6735}}) are listed (p.322-325). }}, both {{ill|lt=ridge tags|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834}} say ``Koimura Hachiman Shrine'' and ``Hachiman Shrine.'' Before {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}, it was located in Genkai Village, Hakusan, west of the current location (currently {{ill|lt=Naka Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1207921|WD=Q1207921|lt=Naka Ward}}{{ill|lt=Shinei|Q11502585|WD=Q11502585}}, Hakusansha), but there is a legend that it was moved to its current location when {{ill|lt=Nagoya Castle|Q648629|WD=Q648629}} was being built over Kiyosu|Q11561274}}. "{{ill|lt=Memorandum of Kanbun Villages|Q137766629|WD=Q137766629}}", which was established in the early Edo period, states, The Kojima family, who were {{ill|lt=Negi|Q11594490|WD=Q11594490}} of Furui village, served as {{ill|lt=Shinto priest|Q15888735|WD=Q15888735}} in {{ill|lt=early modern times|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718}}. {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072}} lord {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Mitsutomo|Q843245|WD=Q843245}} was {{ill|lt=Tenwa (Japan)|Q1145289|WD=Q1145289|lt=Tenwa}}There is a record that the shrine building was repaired in 1683|Q7651}} {{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1981|p=34}}{{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1987|p=429}}. Until {{ill|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535}}, Gyokuryuzan Koshoin (still located to the east of our company) was {{ill|lt=Jinguji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971}}. In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}} (Meiji 5), it was ranked as {{ill|lt=gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} in the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}. The shrine buildings were burnt down in the {{ill|lt=air raid|Q2380335|WD=Q2380335}} of {{ill|lt=Pacific War|Q184425|WD=Q184425}}, but were rebuilt after the war.It was identified by {{ill|lt=Amano Nobukage|Q6535030|WD=Q6535030}} as ``Takamu Shrine'' in Aichi District, Owari Province, which appears in the ``Engishiki Shinmyocho,'' and it became an established theory after the mid-Edo period. In {{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} first year), it was officially renamed "Takamu Shrine". However, the basis for comparing our company to Takamu Shrine is not clear, and {{ill|lt=Masao Tsuda|Q22123474|WD=Q22123474}}, a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=local historian|1=UNKNOWN}} from the late Edo period, questions the idea of ​​comparing our company to ``Takamu Shrine.'' Takamu Shrines other than ours include Takamine Daimyojin (currently enshrined in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yagoto Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}) and Takamu Shrine in {{ill|lt=Takanashi|Q50324950|WD=Q50324950}}, but both of these shrines were built before {{ill|lt=medieval times|Q12554|WD=Q12554}} in {{ill|lt=Yamada District (Owari Province)|Q11470699|WD=Q11470699|lt=Yamada District}} Because it is within the area, it is not considered a likely candidate for Takamu Shrine in Aichi District. Masao Tsuda also lists {{ill|lt=Gokisho Hachimangu Shrine|Q3109957|WD=Q3109957}} as another candidate for Takamu Shrine.From the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q184963}} onward, ``Takamu'' was nicknamed {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=hoko|1=UNKNOWN}}, a symbol of {{ill|lt=weapons|Q728|WD=Q728}}, and a theory spread that it was the site of the armory of the {{ill|lt=Mononobe clan|Q1755836|WD=Q1755836}}, according to the historical site information board in the temple grounds (established by the Nagoya City Board of Education). {{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1981|p=33}}{{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1987|p=428}}, "{{ill|lt=Owari Domestic Shinto Name Book|Q11041830|WD=Q11041830}}" established at the end of the Heian period. Since it is written as ``Takamuku Tenjin,'' it was originally read as ``Takamuku'' and may be related to {{ill|lt=Takamukai clan|Q109357793|WD=Q109357793}} or {{ill|lt=Takamukai Shrine|Q11668968|WD=Q11668968}}. === Enshrined God === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takamimusubi no Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamimusubi no Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}} (Honda Beszon, Hondawake no Mikoto) === Suesha === There are the following subsidiary shrines within the precincts. {{Div col}} * Kitano Tenjin Shrine * Takamuryu Shrine * Tagasha * Kotohirasha * Itsukushimasha * Shinmeisha * Hakusansha * Tsushimasha * Tokugi Inarisha *Soreisha Shrine * Akibasha {{Div col end}} === Others ===``Visiting three shrines of love'' includes {{ill|lt=Yamada Tenmangu|Q3571407|WD=Q3571407}} ({{ill|lt=Kita Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1207964|WD=Q1207964|lt=Kita Ward}}Yamada Town), {{ill|lt=Shiroyama Hachimangu Shrine|Q3482375|WD=Q3482375}} (Chikusa Ward{{ill|lt=Shiroyama Town) (Nagoya City|Q24876539|WD=Q24876539|lt=Shiroyama Town}}), it is a {{ill|lt=pilgrimage|Q1644573|WD=Q1644573}} spot for fulfilling love. |publisher=Nagoya Tourism and Convention Bureau |accessdate=2016-03-18}}{{Cite web|Japanese book|date= |url=http://www.shiroyama.or.jp/sansyameguri.htm |title=Three shrines of love |publisher=Shiroyama Hachimangu Shrine |accessdate=2016-03-18}}. Originally planned in {{ill|lt=2002|Q1987|WD=Q1987}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}}14) as a club activity of {{ill|lt=Ohka Gakuen High School|Q11538202|WD=Q11538202|lt=Ohka Gakuen High School}} It became widely known when {{ill|lt=Central Railway Company|Q513679|WD=Q513679|lt=JR Central}} featured it as a walking event in {{ill|lt=2008|Q2004|WD=Q2004}} (Heisei 20). In addition, there were originally three shrines: {{ill|lt=Nagoya Seimei Shrine|Q17229502|WD=Q17229502}} (Chikusa Ward {{ill|lt=Seimeiyama|Q17221162|WD=Q17221162}}), Takamu Shrine, and Shiroyama Hachiman Shrine. Guri Visiting 3 Shrines to Fulfill Love Planned by Ohka Gakuen High School Students, Event on 26th'', {{ill|lt=Mainichi Shimbun|Q1136866|WD=Q1136866}}Morning edition, May 1, 2007, local edition/Aichi, page 20Natsumi Ikai "Aichi's Archaeological Music, Takamu Shrine, Chikusa Ward, Nagoya City, The Legend of Healing Illness in the Water in the Water", {{ill|lt=Chunichi Shimbun|Q904678|WD=Q904678}}Morning edition, citizen edition, December 13, 2008, p. 19Ken Shiobara, Sansha Get love by visiting? High school students plan, quietly booming in Chikusa, Nagoya”, {{ill|lt=Asahi Shimbun|Q720503|WD=Q720503}} Evening edition, February 3, 2012 (Nagoya headquarters edition), p. 7{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=http://opi-rina.chunichi.co.jp/tankentai/20100128_jinja.html |title=Pray for love fulfillment at three shrines related to love (Opi Exploration Team) |publisher= Chunichi Shimbun Web |date=2010-01-28 |accessdate=2016-03-18}}.There is an old well within the temple grounds, which is still used for {{ill|lt=washing hands|Q1069667|WD=Q1069667}}. It is also the origin of its other name, ``Furui Hachiman,'' as well as the former name of the town where it is enshrined, ``Motofuruimachi,'' and the name of a nearby place, ``Koi no Saka.'' The plaque on the shrine's shrine is based on {{ill|lt=written|written|WD=written|lt=Q11498466}} by {{ill|lt=Nobusuke Kishi|Q315572|WD=Q315572}}. === Location === * {{ill|lt=Aichi "{{ill|lt=Memorandum of Kanbun Villages|Q137766629|WD=Q137766629}}", which was established in the early Edo period, states, The Kojima family, who were {{ill|lt=Negi|Q11594490|WD=Q11594490}} of Furui village, served as {{ill|lt=Shinto priest|Q15888735|WD=Q15888735}} in {{ill|lt=early modern times|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718}}. {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072}} lord {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Mitsutomo|Q843245|WD=Q843245}} was {{ill|lt=Tenwa (Japan)|Q1145289|WD=Q1145289|lt=Tenwa}}There is a record that the shrine building was repaired in 1683|Q7651}} {{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1981|p=34}}{{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1987|p=429}}. Until {{ill|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535}}, Gyokuryuzan Koshoin (still located to the east of our company) was {{ill|lt=Jinguji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971}}. In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}} (Meiji 5), it was ranked as {{ill|lt=gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} in the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}. The shrine buildings were burnt down in the {{ill|lt=air raid|Q2380335|WD=Q2380335}} of {{ill|lt=Pacific War|Q184425|WD=Q184425}}, but were rebuilt after the war.It was identified by {{ill|lt=Amano Nobukage|Q6535030|WD=Q6535030}} as ``Takamu Shrine'' in Aichi District, Owari Province, which appears in the ``Engishiki Shinmyocho,'' and it became an established theory after the mid-Edo period. In {{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} first year), it was officially renamed "Takamu Shrine". However, the basis for comparing our company to Takamu Shrine is not clear, and {{ill|lt=Masao Tsuda|Q22123474|WD=Q22123474}}, a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=local historian|1=UNKNOWN}} from the late Edo period, questions the idea of ​​comparing our company to ``Takamu Shrine.'' Takamu Shrines other than ours include Takamine Daimyojin (currently enshrined in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yagoto Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}) and Takamu Shrine in {{ill|lt=Takanashi|Q50324950|WD=Q50324950}}, but both of these shrines were built before {{ill|lt=medieval times|Q12554|WD=Q12554}} in {{ill|lt=Yamada District (Owari Province)|Q11470699|WD=Q11470699|lt=Yamada District}} Because it is within the area, it is not considered a likely candidate for Takamu Shrine in Aichi District. Masao Tsuda also lists {{ill|lt=Gokisho Hachimangu Shrine|Q3109957|WD=Q3109957}} as another candidate for Takamu Shrine.From the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q184963}} onward, ``Takamu'' was nicknamed {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=hoko|1=UNKNOWN}}, a symbol of {{ill|lt=weapons|Q728|WD=Q728}}, and a theory spread that it was the site of the armory of the {{ill|lt=Mononobe clan|Q1755836|WD=Q1755836}}, according to the historical site information board in the temple grounds (established by the Nagoya City Board of Education). {{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1981|p=33}}{{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1987|p=428}}, "{{ill|lt=Owari Domestic Shinto Name Book|Q11041830|WD=Q11041830}}" established at the end of the Heian period. Since it is written as ``Takamuku Tenjin,'' it was originally read as ``Takamuku'' and may be related to {{ill|lt=Takamukai clan|Q109357793|WD=Q109357793}} or {{ill|lt=Takamukai Shrine|Q11668968|WD=Q11668968}}. === Enshrined God === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takamimusubi no Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamimusubi no Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}} (Honda Beszon, Hondawake no Mikoto) === Suesha === There are the following subsidiary shrines within the precincts. {{Div col}} * Kitano Tenjin Shrine * Takamuryu Shrine * Tagasha * Kotohirasha * Itsukushimasha * Shinmeisha * Hakusansha * Tsushimasha * Tokugi Inarisha *Soreisha Shrine * Akibasha {{Div col end}} === Others ===``Visiting three shrines of love'' includes {{ill|lt=Yamada Tenmangu|Q3571407|WD=Q3571407}} ({{ill|lt=Kita Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1207964|WD=Q1207964|lt=Kita Ward}}Yamada Town), {{ill|lt=Shiroyama Hachimangu Shrine|Q3482375|WD=Q3482375}} (Chikusa Ward{{ill|lt=Shiroyama Town) (Nagoya City|Q24876539|WD=Q24876539|lt=Shiroyama Town}}), it is a {{ill|lt=pilgrimage|Q1644573|WD=Q1644573}} spot for fulfilling love. |publisher=Nagoya Tourism and Convention Bureau |accessdate=2016-03-18}}{{Cite web|Japanese book|date= |url=http://www.shiroyama.or.jp/sansyameguri.htm |title=Three shrines of love |publisher=Shiroyama Hachimangu Shrine |accessdate=2016-03-18}}. Originally planned in {{ill|lt=2002|Q1987|WD=Q1987}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}}14) as a club activity of {{ill|lt=Ohka Gakuen High School|Q11538202|WD=Q11538202|lt=Ohka Gakuen High School}} It became widely known when {{ill|lt=Central Railway Company|Q513679|WD=Q513679|lt=JR Central}} featured it as a walking event in {{ill|lt=2008|Q2004|WD=Q2004}} (Heisei 20). In addition, there were originally three shrines: {{ill|lt=Nagoya Seimei Shrine|Q17229502|WD=Q17229502}} (Chikusa Ward {{ill|lt=Seimeiyama|Q17221162|WD=Q17221162}}), Takamu Shrine, and Shiroyama Hachiman Shrine. Guri Visiting 3 Shrines to Fulfill Love Planned by Ohka Gakuen High School Students, Event on 26th'', {{ill|lt=Mainichi Shimbun|Q1136866|WD=Q1136866}}Morning edition, May 1, 2007, local edition/Aichi, page 20Natsumi Ikai "Aichi's Archaeological Music, Takamu Shrine, Chikusa Ward, Nagoya City, The Legend of Healing Illness in the Water in the Water", {{ill|lt=Chunichi Shimbun|Q904678|WD=Q904678}}Morning edition, citizen edition, December 13, 2008, p. 19Ken Shiobara, Sansha Get love by visiting? High school students plan, quietly booming in Chikusa, Nagoya”, {{ill|lt=Asahi Shimbun|Q720503|WD=Q720503}} Evening edition, February 3, 2012 (Nagoya headquarters edition), p. 7{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=http://opi-rina.chunichi.co.jp/tankentai/20100128_jinja.html |title=Pray for love fulfillment at three shrines related to love (Opi Exploration Team) |publisher= Chunichi Shimbun Web |date=2010-01-28 |accessdate=2016-03-18}}.There is an old well within the temple grounds, which is still used for {{ill|lt=washing hands|Q1069667|WD=Q1069667}}. It is also the origin of its other name, ``Furui Hachiman,'' as well as the former name of the town where it is enshrined, ``Motofuruimachi,'' and the name of a nearby place, ``Koi no Saka.'' Water is drawn from a well in the back and used for washing hands. ]] '''Takamu Shrine'' is located at {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}{{ill|lt=Nagoya City|Q11751|WD=Q11751}}{{ill|lt=Chikusa Ward|Q1207951|WD=Q1207951}}{{ill|lt=Imaike (Nagoya City)|Q11377165|WD=Q11377165|lt=Imaike}} {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in 1-chome. It is compared to {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506}}{{ill|lt=Aichi District (Aichi Prefecture)|Q552687|WD=Q552687|lt=Aichi District}} ``Takamu Shrine'' in ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmecho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}''. === History === According to the precincts information board, it is an old shrine that was founded in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seimu|Q316672|WD=Q316672}}, and that {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}} was enshrined in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243}}. . Until {{ill|lt=early modern times|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718}}, it was called '''Hachimangu''''. It was also called ``Funade Hachiman'' because there was a {{ill|lt=divine statue|Q11588751|WD=Q11588751}} riding on a {{ill|lt=ship|Q1229765|WD=Q1229765}}.After {{ill|lt=1441|Q6402|WD=Q6402}} ({{ill|lt=Kakichi|Q1193238|WD=Q1193238}} first year), there are records of the construction and restoration of multiple shrines. Masao Tsuda also lists {{ill|lt=Gokisho Hachimangu Shrine|Q3109957|WD=Q3109957}} as another candidate for Takamu Shrine.From the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q184963}} onward, ``Takamu'' was nicknamed {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=hoko|1=UNKNOWN}}, a symbol of {{ill|lt=weapons|Q728|WD=Q728}}, and a theory spread that it was the site of the armory of the {{ill|lt=Mononobe clan|Q1755836|WD=Q1755836}}, according to the historical site information board in the temple grounds (established by the Nagoya City Board of Education). {{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1981|p=33}}{{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1987|p=428}}, "{{ill|lt=Owari Domestic Shinto Name Book|Q11041830|WD=Q11041830}}" established at the end of the Heian period. Since it is written as ``Takamuku Tenjin,'' it was originally read as ``Takamuku'' and may be related to {{ill|lt=Takamukai clan|Q109357793|WD=Q109357793}} or {{ill|lt=Takamukai Shrine|Q11668968|WD=Q11668968}}. === Enshrined God === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi no Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamimusubi no Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}} (Honda Beszon, Hondawake no Mikoto) === Suesha === There are the following subsidiary shrines within the precincts. {{Div col}} * Kitano Tenjin Shrine * Takamuryu Shrine * Tagasha * Kotohirasha * Itsukushimasha * Shinmeisha * Hakusansha * Tsushimasha * Tokugi Inarisha *Soreisha Shrine * Akibasha {{Div col end}} === Others ===``Visiting three shrines of love'' includes {{ill|lt=Yamada Tenmangu|Q3571407|WD=Q3571407}} ({{ill|lt=Kita Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1207964|WD=Q1207964|lt=Kita Ward}}Yamada Town), {{ill|lt=Shiroyama Hachimangu Shrine|Q3482375|WD=Q3482375}} (Chikusa Ward{{ill|lt=Shiroyama Town) (Nagoya City|Q24876539|WD=Q24876539|lt=Shiroyama Town}}), it is a {{ill|lt=pilgrimage|Q1644573|WD=Q1644573}} spot for fulfilling love. |publisher=Nagoya Tourism and Convention Bureau |accessdate=2016-03-18}}{{Cite web|Japanese book|date= |url=http://www.shiroyama.or.jp/sansyameguri.htm |title=Three shrines of love |publisher=Shiroyama Hachimangu Shrine |accessdate=2016-03-18}}. Originally planned in {{ill|lt=2002|Q1987|WD=Q1987}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}}14) as a club activity of {{ill|lt=Ohka Gakuen High School|Q11538202|WD=Q11538202|lt=Ohka Gakuen High School}} It became widely known when {{ill|lt=Central Railway Company|Q513679|WD=Q513679|lt=JR Central}} featured it as a walking event in {{ill|lt=2008|Q2004|WD=Q2004}} (Heisei 20). In addition, there were originally three shrines: {{ill|lt=Nagoya Seimei Shrine|Q17229502|WD=Q17229502}} (Chikusa Ward {{ill|lt=Seimeiyama|Q17221162|WD=Q17221162}}), Takamu Shrine, and Shiroyama Hachiman Shrine. Guri Visiting 3 Shrines to Fulfill Love Planned by Ohka Gakuen High School Students, Event on 26th'', {{ill|lt=Mainichi Shimbun|Q1136866|WD=Q1136866}}Morning edition, May 1, 2007, local edition/Aichi, page 20Natsumi Ikai "Aichi's Archaeological Music, Takamu Shrine, Chikusa Ward, Nagoya City, The Legend of Healing Illness in the Water in the Water", {{ill|lt=Chunichi Shimbun|Q904678|WD=Q904678}}Morning edition, citizen edition, December 13, 2008, p. 19Ken Shiobara, Sansha Get love by visiting? High school students plan, quietly booming in Chikusa, Nagoya”, {{ill|lt=Asahi Shimbun|Q720503|WD=Q720503}} Evening edition, February 3, 2012 (Nagoya headquarters edition), p. 7{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=http://opi-rina.chunichi.co.jp/tankentai/20100128_jinja.html |title=Pray for love fulfillment at three shrines related to love (Opi Exploration Team) |publisher= Chunichi Shimbun Web |date=2010-01-28 |accessdate=2016-03-18}}.There is an old well within the temple grounds, which is still used for {{ill|lt=washing hands|Q1069667|WD=Q1069667}}. It is also the origin of its other name, ``Furui Hachiman,'' as well as the former name of the town where it is enshrined, ``Motofuruimachi,'' and the name of a nearby place, ``Koi no Saka.'' Water is drawn from a well in the back and used for washing hands. ]] '''Takamu Shrine'' is located at {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}{{ill|lt=Nagoya City|Q11751|WD=Q11751}}{{ill|lt=Chikusa Ward|Q1207951|WD=Q1207951}}{{ill|lt=Imaike (Nagoya City)|Q11377165|WD=Q11377165|lt=Imaike}} {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in 1-chome. It is compared to {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506}}{{ill|lt=Aichi District (Aichi Prefecture)|Q552687|WD=Q552687|lt=Aichi District}} ``Takamu Shrine'' in ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmecho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}''. === History === According to the precincts information board, it is an old shrine that was founded in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seimu|Q316672|WD=Q316672}}, and that {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}} was enshrined in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243}}. . Until {{ill|lt=early modern times|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718}}, it was called '''Hachimangu''''. It was also called ``Funade Hachiman'' because there was a {{ill|lt=divine statue|Q11588751|WD=Q11588751}} riding on a {{ill|lt=ship|Q1229765|WD=Q1229765}}.After {{ill|lt=1441|Q6402|WD=Q6402}} ({{ill|lt=Kakichi|Q1193238|WD=Q1193238}} first year), there are records of the construction and restoration of multiple shrines. The Shrines and Temples Edition includes the {{ill|lt=Kansho|Q1143928|WD=Q1143928}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1461|Q6532|WD=Q6532}}) and {{ill|lt=astronomical studies that had been handed down at the time of compilation. (era name)|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|lt=astronomical}} 1st year ({{ill|lt=1532|Q6436|WD=Q6436}}), {{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|WD=Q1143341}}8th year ({{ill|lt=156 5th year|Q6634|WD=Q6634}}) and {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}8th year ({{ill|lt=1603|Q6735|WD=Q6735}}) are listed (p.322-325). }}, both {{ill|lt=ridge tags|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834}} say ``Koimura Hachiman Shrine'' and ``Hachiman Shrine.'' Before {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}, it was located in Genkai Village, Hakusan, west of the current location (currently {{ill|lt=Naka Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1207921|WD=Q1207921|lt=Naka Ward}}{{ill|lt=Shinei|Q11502585|WD=Q11502585}}, Hakusansha), but there is a legend that it was moved to its current location when {{ill|lt=Nagoya Castle|Q648629|WD=Q648629}} was being built over Kiyosu|Q11561274}}. "{{ill|lt=Memorandum of Kanbun Villages|Q137766629|WD=Q137766629}}", which was established in the early Edo period, states, The Kojima family, who were {{ill|lt=Negi|Q11594490|WD=Q11594490}} of Furui village, served as {{ill|lt=Shinto priest|Q15888735|WD=Q15888735}} in {{ill|lt=early modern times|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718}}. {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072}} lord {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Mitsutomo|Q843245|WD=Q843245}} was {{ill|lt=Tenwa (Japan)|Q1145289|WD=Q1145289|lt=Tenwa}}There is a record that the shrine building was repaired in 1683|Q7651}} {{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1981|p=34}}{{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1987|p=429}}. Until {{ill|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535}}, Gyokuryuzan Koshoin (still located to the east of our company) was {{ill|lt=Jinguji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971}}. In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}} (Meiji 5), it was ranked as {{ill|lt=gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} in the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}. The shrine buildings were burnt down in the {{ill|lt=air raid|Q2380335|WD=Q2380335}} of {{ill|lt=Pacific War|Q184425|WD=Q184425}}, but were rebuilt after the war.It was identified by {{ill|lt=Amano Nobukage|Q6535030|WD=Q6535030}} as ``Takamu Shrine'' in Aichi District, Owari Province, which appears in the ``Engishiki Shinmyocho,'' and it became an established theory after the mid-Edo period. In {{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} first year), it was officially renamed "Takamu Shrine". However, the basis for comparing our company to Takamu Shrine is not clear, and {{ill|lt=Masao Tsuda|Q22123474|WD=Q22123474}}, a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=local historian|1=UNKNOWN}} from the late Edo period, questions the idea of ​​comparing our company to ``Takamu Shrine.'' In addition, there were originally three shrines: {{ill|lt=Nagoya Seimei Shrine|Q17229502|WD=Q17229502}} (Chikusa Ward {{ill|lt=Seimeiyama|Q17221162|WD=Q17221162}}), Takamu Shrine, and Shiroyama Hachiman Shrine. Water is drawn from a well in the back and used for washing hands. ]] '''Takamu Shrine'' is located at {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}{{ill|lt=Nagoya City|Q11751|WD=Q11751}}{{ill|lt=Chikusa Ward|Q1207951|WD=Q1207951}}{{ill|lt=Imaike (Nagoya City)|Q11377165|WD=Q11377165|lt=Imaike}} {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in 1-chome. It is compared to {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506}}{{ill|lt=Aichi District (Aichi Prefecture)|Q552687|WD=Q552687|lt=Aichi District}} ``Takamu Shrine'' in ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmecho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}''. === History === According to the precincts information board, it is an old shrine that was founded in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seimu|Q316672|WD=Q316672}}, and that {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}} was enshrined in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243}}. . Until {{ill|lt=early modern times|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718}}, it was called '''Hachimangu''''. It was also called ``Funade Hachiman'' because there was a {{ill|lt=divine statue|Q11588751|WD=Q11588751}} riding on a {{ill|lt=ship|Q1229765|WD=Q1229765}}.After {{ill|lt=1441|Q6402|WD=Q6402}} ({{ill|lt=Kakichi|Q1193238|WD=Q1193238}} first year), there are records of the construction and restoration of multiple shrines. The Shrines and Temples Edition includes the {{ill|lt=Kansho|Q1143928|WD=Q1143928}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1461|Q6532|WD=Q6532}}) and {{ill|lt=astronomical studies that had been handed down at the time of compilation. (era name)|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|lt=astronomical}} 1st year ({{ill|lt=1532|Q6436|WD=Q6436}}), {{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|WD=Q1143341}}8th year ({{ill|lt=156 5th year|Q6634|WD=Q6634}}) and {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}8th year ({{ill|lt=1603|Q6735|WD=Q6735}}) are listed (p.322-325). }}, both {{ill|lt=ridge tags|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834}} say ``Koimura Hachiman Shrine'' and ``Hachiman Shrine.'' Before {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}, it was located in Genkai Village, Hakusan, west of the current location (currently {{ill|lt=Naka Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1207921|WD=Q1207921|lt=Naka Ward}}{{ill|lt=Shinei|Q11502585|WD=Q11502585}}, Hakusansha), but there is a legend that it was moved to its current location when {{ill|lt=Nagoya Castle|Q648629|WD=Q648629}} was being built over Kiyosu|Q11561274}}. "{{ill|lt=Memorandum of Kanbun Villages|Q137766629|WD=Q137766629}}", which was established in the early Edo period, states, The Kojima family, who were {{ill|lt=Negi|Q11594490|WD=Q11594490}} of Furui village, served as {{ill|lt=Shinto priest|Q15888735|WD=Q15888735}} in {{ill|lt=early modern times|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718}}. {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072}} lord {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Mitsutomo|Q843245|WD=Q843245}} was {{ill|lt=Tenwa (Japan)|Q1145289|WD=Q1145289|lt=Tenwa}}There is a record that the shrine building was repaired in 1683|Q7651}} {{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1981|p=34}}{{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1987|p=429}}. Until {{ill|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535}}, Gyokuryuzan Koshoin (still located to the east of our company) was {{ill|lt=Jinguji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971}}. In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}} (Meiji 5), it was ranked as {{ill|lt=gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} in the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}. The shrine buildings were burnt down in the {{ill|lt=air raid|Q2380335|WD=Q2380335}} of {{ill|lt=Pacific War|Q184425|WD=Q184425}}, but were rebuilt after the war.It was identified by {{ill|lt=Amano Nobukage|Q6535030|WD=Q6535030}} as ``Takamu Shrine'' in Aichi District, Owari Province, which appears in the ``Engishiki Shinmyocho,'' and it became an established theory after the mid-Edo period. In {{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} first year), it was officially renamed "Takamu Shrine". However, the basis for comparing our company to Takamu Shrine is not clear, and {{ill|lt=Masao Tsuda|Q22123474|WD=Q22123474}}, a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=local historian|1=UNKNOWN}} from the late Edo period, questions the idea of ​​comparing our company to ``Takamu Shrine.'' Takamu Shrines other than ours include Takamine Daimyojin (currently enshrined in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yagoto Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}) and Takamu Shrine in {{ill|lt=Takanashi|Q50324950|WD=Q50324950}}, but both of these shrines were built before {{ill|lt=medieval times|Q12554|WD=Q12554}} in {{ill|lt=Yamada District (Owari Province)|Q11470699|WD=Q11470699|lt=Yamada District}} Because it is within the area, it is not considered a likely candidate for Takamu Shrine in Aichi District. Masao Tsuda also lists {{ill|lt=Gokisho Hachimangu Shrine|Q3109957|WD=Q3109957}} as another candidate for Takamu Shrine.From the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q184963}} onward, ``Takamu'' was nicknamed {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=hoko|1=UNKNOWN}}, a symbol of {{ill|lt=weapons|Q728|WD=Q728}}, and a theory spread that it was the site of the armory of the {{ill|lt=Mononobe clan|Q1755836|WD=Q1755836}}, according to the historical site information board in the temple grounds (established by the Nagoya City Board of Education). {{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1981|p=33}}{{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1987|p=428}}, "{{ill|lt=Owari Domestic Shinto Name Book|Q11041830|WD=Q11041830}}" established at the end of the Heian period. Since it is written as ``Takamuku Tenjin,'' it was originally read as ``Takamuku'' and may be related to {{ill|lt=Takamukai clan|Q109357793|WD=Q109357793}} or {{ill|lt=Takamukai Shrine|Q11668968|WD=Q11668968}}. === Enshrined God === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi no Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q3275901|lt=Kamimusubi no Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}} (Honda Beszon, Hondawake no Mikoto) === Suesha === There are the following subsidiary shrines within the precincts. {{Div col}} * Kitano Tenjin Shrine * Takamuryu Shrine * Tagasha * Kotohirasha * Itsukushimasha * Shinmeisha * Hakusansha * Tsushimasha * Tokugi Inarisha *Soreisha Shrine * Akibasha {{Div col end}} === Others ===``Visiting three shrines of love'' includes {{ill|lt=Yamada Tenmangu|Q3571407|WD=Q3571407}} ({{ill|lt=Kita Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1207964|WD=Q1207964|lt=Kita Ward}}Yamada Town), {{ill|lt=Shiroyama Hachimangu Shrine|Q3482375|WD=Q3482375}} (Chikusa Ward{{ill|lt=Shiroyama Town) (Nagoya City|Q24876539|WD=Q24876539|lt=Shiroyama Town}}), it is a {{ill|lt=pilgrimage|Q1644573|WD=Q1644573}} spot for fulfilling love. |publisher=Nagoya Tourism and Convention Bureau |accessdate=2016-03-18}}{{Cite web|Japanese book|date= |url=http://www.shiroyama.or.jp/sansyameguri.htm |title=Three shrines of love |publisher=Shiroyama Hachimangu Shrine |accessdate=2016-03-18}}. Hachiman Shrine|Regular Festival = {{ill|lt=October 14th|Q2922|WD=Q2922|1=October 14}}-{{ill|lt=October 15th|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = Nagoya }} [[File:Takamu shrine, Plaque of shrine name (Chikusa ward, Nagoya city. 2016.03.17).jpg|thumb|220px|right|The shrine name plaque on the shrine. At the bottom left is written ``Kishi Nobusuke.'' ]] [[File:Chikusa Takamu Shrine 2009-08B.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Torii of Tokugi Inari Shrine]] [[File:Takamu Shrine, Chōzuya (Chikusa ward, Nagoya city. 2016.03.02).JPG|thumb|220px|right|Chozuya at Takamu Shrine. Water is drawn from a well in the back and used for washing hands. ]] '''Takamu Shrine'' is located at {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Nagoya City|Q11751|WD=Q11751|1=Nagoya}}{{ill|lt=Chikusa Ward|Q1207951|WD=Q1207951|1=Chikusa-ku, Nagoya}}{{ill|lt=Imaike (Nagoya City)|Q11377165|WD=Q11377165|lt=Imaike|1=Imaike}} {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in 1-chome. It is compared to {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506|1=Owari Province}}{{ill|lt=Aichi District (Aichi Prefecture)|Q552687|WD=Q552687|lt=Aichi District|1=Aichi District}} ``Takamu Shrine'' in ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmecho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}''. === History === According to the precincts information board, it is an old shrine that was founded in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seimu|Q316672|WD=Q316672|1=Emperor Seimu}}, and that {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}} was enshrined in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243|1=Emperor Seiwa}}. . Until {{ill|lt=early modern times|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718|1=Early modern period}}, it was called '''Hachimangu''''. It was also called ``Funade Hachiman'' because there was a {{ill|lt=divine statue|Q11588751|WD=Q11588751|1=Idols in Shinto}} riding on a {{ill|lt=ship|Q1229765|WD=Q1229765|1=Watercraft}}.After {{ill|lt=1441|Q6402|WD=Q6402|1=1441}} ({{ill|lt=Kakichi|Q1193238|WD=Q1193238|1=Kakitsu}} first year), there are records of the construction and restoration of multiple shrines. The Shrines and Temples Edition includes the {{ill|lt=Kansho|Q1143928|WD=Q1143928|1=Kanshō}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1461|Q6532|WD=Q6532|1=1461}}) and {{ill|lt=astronomical studies that had been handed down at the time of compilation. (era name)|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|lt=astronomical|1=Tenbun}} 1st year ({{ill|lt=1532|Q6436|WD=Q6436|1=1532}}), {{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|WD=Q1143341|1=Eiroku}}8th year ({{ill|lt=156 5th year|Q6634|WD=Q6634|1=1565}}) and {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}}8th year ({{ill|lt=1603|Q6735|WD=Q6735|1=1603}}) are listed (p.322-325). }}, both {{ill|lt=ridge tags|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834|1=Munafuda}} say ``Koimura Hachiman Shrine'' and ``Hachiman Shrine.'' Before {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}}, it was located in Genkai Village, Hakusan, west of the current location (currently {{ill|lt=Naka Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1207921|WD=Q1207921|lt=Naka Ward|1=Naka-ku, Nagoya}}{{ill|lt=Shinei|Q11502585|WD=Q11502585|1=Shinei}}, Hakusansha), but there is a legend that it was moved to its current location when {{ill|lt=Nagoya Castle|Q648629|WD=Q648629|1=Nagoya Castle}} was being built over Kiyosu|Q11561274}}. "{{ill|lt=Memorandum of Kanbun Villages|Q137766629|WD=Q137766629|1=Kanbunmuramuraoboegaki}}", which was established in the early Edo period, states, The Kojima family, who were {{ill|lt=Negi|Q11594490|WD=Q11594490|1=Negi}} of Furui village, served as {{ill|lt=Shinto priest|Q15888735|WD=Q15888735|1=Kannushi (disambiguation)}} in {{ill|lt=early modern times|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718|1=Early modern period}}. {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}} lord {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Mitsutomo|Q843245|WD=Q843245|1=Tokugawa Mitsutomo}} was {{ill|lt=Tenwa (Japan)|Q1145289|WD=Q1145289|lt=Tenwa|1=Tenna}}There is a record that the shrine building was repaired in 1683|Q7651}} {{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1981|p=34}}{{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1987|p=429}}. Until {{ill|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535|1=Shinbutsu bunri}}, Gyokuryuzan Koshoin (still located to the east of our company) was {{ill|lt=Jinguji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971|1=Jingū-ji}}. In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} (Meiji 5), it was ranked as {{ill|lt=gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} in the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. The shrine buildings were burnt down in the {{ill|lt=air raid|Q2380335|WD=Q2380335|1=Airstrike}} of {{ill|lt=Pacific War|Q184425|WD=Q184425|1=Pacific War}}, but were rebuilt after the war.It was identified by {{ill|lt=Amano Nobukage|Q6535030|WD=Q6535030|1=Amano Sadakage}} as ``Takamu Shrine'' in Aichi District, Owari Province, which appears in the ``Engishiki Shinmyocho,'' and it became an established theory after the mid-Edo period. In {{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717|1=1868}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} first year), it was officially renamed "Takamu Shrine". However, the basis for comparing our company to Takamu Shrine is not clear, and {{ill|lt=Masao Tsuda|Q22123474|WD=Q22123474|1=Tsuda Masanari}}, a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=local historian|1=UNKNOWN}} from the late Edo period, questions the idea of ​​comparing our company to ``Takamu Shrine.'' Takamu Shrines other than ours include Takamine Daimyojin (currently enshrined in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yagoto Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}) and Takamu Shrine in {{ill|lt=Takanashi|Q50324950|WD=Q50324950|1=Takabari}}, but both of these shrines were built before {{ill|lt=medieval times|Q12554|WD=Q12554|1=Middle Ages}} in {{ill|lt=Yamada District (Owari Province)|Q11470699|WD=Q11470699|lt=Yamada District|1=Yamada district, Owari}} Because it is within the area, it is not considered a likely candidate for Takamu Shrine in Aichi District. Masao Tsuda also lists {{ill|lt=Gokisho Hachimangu Shrine|Q3109957|WD=Q3109957|1=Gokiso Hachimangū}} as another candidate for Takamu Shrine.From the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period|WD=Q184963}} onward, ``Takamu'' was nicknamed {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=hoko|1=UNKNOWN}}, a symbol of {{ill|lt=weapons|Q728|WD=Q728|1=Weapon}}, and a theory spread that it was the site of the armory of the {{ill|lt=Mononobe clan|Q1755836|WD=Q1755836|1=Mononobe clan}}, according to the historical site information board in the temple grounds (established by the Nagoya City Board of Education). {{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1981|p=33}}{{Sfn|History of Chikusa Ward|1987|p=428}}, "{{ill|lt=Owari Domestic Shinto Name Book|Q11041830|WD=Q11041830|1=Owari Kokunai Jinmyōchō}}" established at the end of the Heian period. Since it is written as ``Takamuku Tenjin,'' it was originally read as ``Takamuku'' and may be related to {{ill|lt=Takamukai clan|Q109357793|WD=Q109357793|1=Takamukai clan}} or {{ill|lt=Takamukai Shrine|Q11668968|WD=Q11668968|1=Takamuku Shrine}}. === Enshrined God === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi no Kami|1=Takamimusubi}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q3275901|lt=Kamimusubi no Kami|1=Kamimusubi}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}} (Honda Beszon, Hondawake no Mikoto) === Suesha === There are the following subsidiary shrines within the precincts. {{Div col}} * Kitano Tenjin Shrine * Takamuryu Shrine * Tagasha * Kotohirasha * Itsukushimasha * Shinmeisha * Hakusansha * Tsushimasha * Tokugi Inarisha *Soreisha Shrine * Akibasha {{Div col end}} === Others ===``Visiting three shrines of love'' includes {{ill|lt=Yamada Tenmangu|Q3571407|WD=Q3571407|1=Yamada Tenmangū}} ({{ill|lt=Kita Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1207964|WD=Q1207964|lt=Kita Ward|1=Kita-ku, Nagoya}}Yamada Town), {{ill|lt=Shiroyama Hachimangu Shrine|Q3482375|WD=Q3482375|1=Shiroyama Hachimangū}} (Chikusa Ward{{ill|lt=Shiroyama Town) (Nagoya City|Q24876539|WD=Q24876539|lt=Shiroyama Town|1=Shiroyama Town}}), it is a {{ill|lt=pilgrimage|Q1644573|WD=Q1644573|1=Pilgrimage}} spot for fulfilling love. |publisher=Nagoya Tourism and Convention Bureau |accessdate=2016-03-18}}{{Cite web|Japanese book|date= |url=http://www.shiroyama.or.jp/sansyameguri.htm |title=Three shrines of love |publisher=Shiroyama Hachimangu Shrine |accessdate=2016-03-18}}. Originally planned in {{ill|lt=2002|Q1987|WD=Q1987|1=2002}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}}14) as a club activity of {{ill|lt=Ohka Gakuen High School|Q11538202|WD=Q11538202|lt=Ohka Gakuen High School|1=Ohka Gakuen High School}} It became widely known when {{ill|lt=Central Railway Company|Q513679|WD=Q513679|lt=JR Central|1=Central Japan Railway Company}} featured it as a walking event in {{ill|lt=2008|Q2004|WD=Q2004|1=2008}} (Heisei 20). In addition, there were originally three shrines: {{ill|lt=Nagoya Seimei Shrine|Q17229502|WD=Q17229502|1=Nagoya Seimei Shrine}} (Chikusa Ward {{ill|lt=Seimeiyama|Q17221162|WD=Q17221162|1=Seimeiyama}}), Takamu Shrine, and Shiroyama Hachiman Shrine. Guri Visiting 3 Shrines to Fulfill Love Planned by Ohka Gakuen High School Students, Event on 26th'', {{ill|lt=Mainichi Shimbun|Q1136866|WD=Q1136866|1=Mainichi Shimbun}}Morning edition, May 1, 2007, local edition/Aichi, page 20Natsumi Ikai "Aichi's Archaeological Music, Takamu Shrine, Chikusa Ward, Nagoya City, The Legend of Healing Illness in the Water in the Water", {{ill|lt=Chunichi Shimbun|Q904678|WD=Q904678|1=Chunichi Shimbun}}Morning edition, citizen edition, December 13, 2008, p. 19Ken Shiobara, Sansha Get love by visiting? High school students plan, quietly booming in Chikusa, Nagoya”, {{ill|lt=Asahi Shimbun|Q720503|WD=Q720503|1=The Asahi Shimbun}} Evening edition, February 3, 2012 (Nagoya headquarters edition), p. 7{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=http://opi-rina.chunichi.co.jp/tankentai/20100128_jinja.html |title=Pray for love fulfillment at three shrines related to love (Opi Exploration Team) |publisher= Chunichi Shimbun Web |date=2010-01-28 |accessdate=2016-03-18}}.There is an old well within the temple grounds, which is still used for {{ill|lt=washing hands|Q1069667|WD=Q1069667|1=Chōzuya}}. It is also the origin of its other name, ``Furui Hachiman,'' as well as the former name of the town where it is enshrined, ``Motofuruimachi,'' and the name of a nearby place, ``Koi no Saka.'' The plaque on the shrine's shrine is based on {{ill|lt=written|written|WD=written|ltq=Q11498466}} by {{ill|lt=Nobusuke Kishi|Q315572|WD=Q315572|1=Nobusuke Kishi}}. === Location === * {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Nagoya City|Q11751|WD=Q11751|1=Nagoya}}{{ill|lt=Chikusa Ward|Q1207951|WD=Q1207951|1=Chikusa-ku, Nagoya}}{{ill|lt=Imaike (Nagoya City)|Q11377165|WD=Q11377165|lt=Imaike|1=Imaike}}1-4-18 === Access === * {{ill|lt=Central Railway|Q513679|WD=Q513679|lt=JR|1=Central Japan Railway Company}}{{ill|lt=Chuo Line (Nagoya area)|Q11363767|WD=Q11363767|lt=Chuo Main Line|1=Chūō line}}・{{ill|lt=Nagoya Municipal Subway|Q1144874|WD=Q1144874|1=Nagoya Municipal Subway}}{{jalink| 150m southeast of {{ill|lt=Chikusa Station|Q863068|WD=Q863068|1=Chikusa Station}} on the Nagoya Municipal Subway Higashiyama Line|Q1132799|Higashiyama Line}}. * 200m west of {{ill|lt=Imaike Station (Aichi Prefecture)|Q852173|WD=Q852173|lt=Imaike Station|1=Imaike Station (Nagoya)}} of Nagoya Municipal Subway Higashiyama Line/Nagoya Municipal Subway {{ill|lt=Nagoya Municipal Subway Sakuradori
